We study this phenomenon by treating uncertainty as a controlled variable: we inject structured perturbations into artifact-derived representations, execute fixed workflows under comprehensive logging, and quantify contamination via trace divergence in plans, tool invocations, and intermediate state. Across 614 paired runs on 32 GAIA tasks with three different language models, we find a decoupling: workflows may diverge substantially yet recover correct answers, or remain structurally similar while producing incorrect outputs. We characterize three manifestation types: silent semantic corruption, behavioral detours with recovery, and combined structural disruption and their control-flow signatures (rerouting, extended execution, early termination). We measure operational costs and characterize why commonly used verification guardrails fail to intercept contamination. We contribute (i) a formal taxonomy of contamination manifestations in structured workflows, (ii) a trace-based measurement framework for detecting and localizing contamination across agent interactions, and (iii) empirical evidence with implications for targeted verification, defensive design, and cost control.

AI.tldr: Agent workflows over heterogeneous documents can be disrupted by uncertainty in artifact representations, causing trace divergence even when answers remain correct, with distinct contamination patterns that bypass typical verification guardrails.
AI.motivation: Increasing use of structured agent workflows for reasoning over heterogeneous artifacts (PDFs, spreadsheets, slides) creates systems where uncertainty is not just about input quality but affects workflow decomposition, routing decisions, intermediate state, and execution trajectories.
AI.method: Treat uncertainty as a controlled variable by injecting structured perturbations into artifact-derived representations, executing fixed workflows with comprehensive logging, and quantifying contamination via trace divergence in plans, tool invocations, and intermediate state across 614 paired runs on 32 GAIA tasks with three different language models.
AI.result: Found decoupling: workflows can diverge substantially yet recover correct answers, or remain structurally similar while producing incorrect outputs. Characterized three contamination types: silent semantic corruption, behavioral detours with recovery, and combined structural disruption with their control-flow signatures (rerouting, extended execution, early termination). Measured operational costs and identified why common verification guardrails fail to intercept contamination.
AI.conclusion: Contributions include a formal taxonomy of contamination manifestations, a trace-based measurement framework for detecting and localizing contamination across agent interactions, and empirical evidence supporting targeted verification, defensive design, and cost control for structured agent workflows over heterogeneous artifacts.

title: Toward Scalable SDN for LEO Mega-Constellations: A Graph Learning Approach
summary: Terrestrial network limitations drive the integration of non-terrestrial networks (NTNs), notably mega-constellations comprising thousands of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites. While these satellites act as interconnected network switches via inter-satellite links (ISLs), their massive scale creates severe bottlenecks for network management. To address this, we propose a scalable, hierarchical software-defined networking (SDN) framework. Our architecture leverages graph neural networks (GNNs) to compactly represent the constellation topology, and Koopman theory to linearize nonlinear dynamics. Specifically, a Graph Koopman Autoencoder (GKAE) forecasts spatio-temporal behavior within a linear subspace for each orbital shell. A central SDN controller then aggregates these shell-level predictions for globally coordinated control. Simulations on the Starlink constellation demonstrate that our approach achieves at least a 42.8\% improvement in spatial compression and a 10.81\% improvement in temporal forecasting compared to established baselines, all while utilizing a significantly smaller model footprint.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical SDN framework using Graph Koopman Autoencoders for scalable management of mega-constellation satellite networks, achieving significant improvements in compression and forecasting with smaller models.
AI.motivation: The massive scale of mega-constellations (thousands of LEO satellites) creates severe bottlenecks for network management in integrated terrestrial-non-terrestrial networks, requiring scalable solutions for handling interconnected satellite networks.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical SDN framework that uses Graph Neural Networks to compactly represent constellation topology and Koopman theory to linearize nonlinear dynamics. A Graph Koopman Autoencoder forecasts spatio-temporal behavior within linear subspaces for each orbital shell, with a central SDN controller aggregating shell-level predictions.
AI.result: Simulations on the Starlink constellation show the approach achieves at least 42.8% improvement in spatial compression and 10.81% improvement in temporal forecasting compared to baselines, while using a significantly smaller model footprint.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical SDN framework with Graph Koopman Autoencoders provides a scalable solution for managing massive satellite constellations, overcoming network management bottlenecks through efficient spatio-temporal forecasting and coordination.

title: Math Education Digital Shadows for facilitating learning with LLMs: Math performance, anxiety and confidence in simulated students and AIs
summary: To enhance LLMs' impact on math education, we need data on their mathematical prowess and biases across prompts. To fill this gap, we introduce MEDS (Math Education Digital Shadows) as a dataset mapping how large language models reason about and report mathematics across human- and AI-like conditions. MEDS involves 28,000 personas from 14 LLMs (from families like Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Granite, Phi and Grok) shadowing either humans or AI assistants. Each record/shadow includes a set of prompts along with psychological/sociodemographic persona metadata and four types of math tasks: (i) open math interview, (ii) three psychometric tests about math perceptions with explanations, (iii) cognitive networks capturing math attitudes, and (iv) 18 high-school math test questions together with their reasoning and confidence scores. MEDS differs from traditional score-only math benchmarks because it integrates concepts of self-efficacy, math anxiety, and cognitive network science besides math proficiency scores. Data validation shows that the sampled LLMs exhibit schema integrity and consistent personas, together with family-specific peculiarities like human-like negative math attitudes, logical fallacies, and math overconfidence. MEDS will benefit learning analytics experts, cognitive scientists, and developers of safer AI tutors in mathematics.

AI.tldr: MEDS dataset maps how LLMs reason about and report mathematics across human- and AI-like conditions, integrating math proficiency with psychological factors like self-efficacy and anxiety.
AI.motivation: To understand LLMs' mathematical prowess and biases across prompts to enhance their impact on math education, filling the gap in data about how they reason and report mathematics.
AI.method: Created MEDS dataset with 28,000 personas from 14 LLMs (Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Granite, Phi, Grok families) shadowing humans or AI assistants. Each record includes prompts with persona metadata and four math task types: open math interview, psychometric tests with explanations, cognitive networks for math attitudes, and 18 high-school math test questions with reasoning and confidence scores.
AI.result: LLMs exhibit schema integrity and consistent personas with family-specific peculiarities including human-like negative math attitudes, logical fallacies, and math overconfidence. The dataset integrates math proficiency with psychological factors unlike traditional score-only benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MEDS will benefit learning analytics experts, cognitive scientists, and developers of safer AI tutors in mathematics by providing comprehensive data on LLM mathematical reasoning that includes both cognitive and affective dimensions.

title: Low Rank Adaptation for Adversarial Perturbation
summary:
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), which leverages the insight that model updates typically reside in a low-dimensional space, has significantly improved the training efficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs) by updating neural network layers using low-rank matrices. Since the generation of adversarial examples is an optimization process analogous to model training, this naturally raises the question: Do adversarial perturbations exhibit a similar low-rank structure?
  In this paper, we provide both theoretical analysis and extensive empirical investigation across various attack methods, model architectures, and datasets to show that adversarial perturbations indeed possess an inherently low-rank structure. This insight opens up new opportunities for improving both adversarial attacks and defenses. We mainly focus on leveraging this low-rank property to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of black-box adversarial attacks, which often suffer from excessive query requirements. Our method follows a two-step approach. First, we use a reference model and auxiliary data to guide the projection of gradients into a low-dimensional subspace. Next, we confine the perturbation search in black-box attacks to this low-rank subspace, significantly improving the efficiency and effectiveness of the adversarial attacks.
  We evaluated our approach across a range of attack methods, benchmark models, datasets, and threat models. The results demonstrate substantial and consistent improvements in the performance of our low-rank adversarial attacks compared to conventional methods.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether adversarial perturbations exhibit low-rank structure similar to model updates in LoRA, and leverages this property to improve black-box adversarial attack efficiency by constraining perturbations to a low-dimensional subspace.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from observing that Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) improves LLM training efficiency by exploiting the low-rank structure of model updates. Since adversarial example generation is an optimization process similar to model training, the authors investigate whether adversarial perturbations also exhibit inherent low-rank structure, which could lead to improvements in both adversarial attacks and defenses.
AI.method: The method follows a two-step approach: (1) Using a reference model and auxiliary data to guide gradient projection into a low-dimensional subspace, and (2) Confining perturbation search in black-box attacks to this low-rank subspace to improve attack efficiency and effectiveness.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation across various attack methods, benchmark models, datasets, and threat models demonstrates substantial and consistent improvements in attack performance compared to conventional methods, confirming that adversarial perturbations indeed possess inherently low-rank structure.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial perturbations exhibit a similar low-rank structure to model updates in LoRA, and leveraging this property can significantly improve the efficiency and effectiveness of black-box adversarial attacks by reducing query requirements and focusing search in low-dimensional subspaces.

title: WaferSAGE: Large Language Model-Powered Wafer Defect Analysis via Synthetic Data Generation and Rubric-Guided Reinforcement Learning
summary:
We present WaferSAGE, a framework for wafer defect visual question answering using small vision-language models. To address data scarcity in semiconductor manufacturing, we propose a three-stage synthesis pipeline incorporating structured rubric generation for precise evaluation. Starting from limited labeled wafer maps, we employ clustering-based cleaning to filter label noise, then generate comprehensive defect descriptions using vision-language models, which are converted into structured evaluation rubrics criteria. These rubrics guide the synthesis of VQA pairs, ensuring coverage across defect type identification, spatial distribution, morphology, and root cause analysis.
  Our dual assessment framework aligns rule-based metrics with LLM-Judge scores via Bayesian optimization, enabling reliable automated evaluation. Through curriculum-based reinforcement learning with Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) and rubric-aligned rewards, our 4B-parameter Qwen3-VL model achieves a 6.493 LLM-Judge score, closely approaching Gemini-3-Flash (7.149) while enabling complete on-premise deployment. We demonstrate that small models with domain-specific training can surpass proprietary large models in specialized industrial visual understanding, offering a viable path for privacy-preserving, cost-effective deployment in semiconductor manufacturing.

AI.tldr: WaferSAGE is a framework for wafer defect visual question answering using small vision-language models, achieving near-proprietary model performance through domain-specific training while enabling on-premise deployment.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity in semiconductor manufacturing for wafer defect analysis while enabling privacy-preserving, cost-effective deployment through small models instead of relying on proprietary large models.
AI.method: Three-stage synthesis pipeline: 1) clustering-based cleaning of wafer maps, 2) generation of defect descriptions using vision-language models converted to structured rubrics, 3) synthesis of VQA pairs guided by rubrics. Uses dual assessment framework with Bayesian optimization and curriculum-based reinforcement learning with Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO).
AI.result: 4B-parameter Qwen3-VL model achieves 6.493 LLM-Judge score, closely approaching Gemini-3-Flash's 7.149 score while enabling complete on-premise deployment, demonstrating small models can surpass proprietary large models in specialized industrial tasks.
AI.conclusion: Small vision-language models with domain-specific training can outperform proprietary large models in specialized industrial visual understanding, offering a viable path for privacy-preserving, cost-effective deployment in semiconductor manufacturing.

title: FMCL: Class-Aware Client Clustering with Foundation Model Representations for Heterogeneous Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients without sharing raw data, yet its performance deteriorates under statistical heterogeneity. Clustered Federated Learning addresses this challenge by grouping similar clients and training separate models per cluster. However, existing clustering strategies often rely on raw data statistics, model parameters, or heuristic similarity measures that fail to capture class-level semantic structure across heterogeneous domains and frequently require iterative coordination. We propose FMCL, a one-shot, class-aware client clustering framework that leverages foundation model representations to construct semantic client signatures. Using a frozen foundation model, FMCL computes class-level embedding prototypes for each client and measures similarity via cosine distance between their class-aware representations. Clustering is performed once prior to training, introducing no additional communication during federated optimization and remaining agnostic to the downstream model architecture. Extensive experiments across heterogeneous benchmarks demonstrate that FMCL improves federated performance and yields more stable clustering behavior compared to existing clustering-based methods under non-identically distributed data partitioning.

AI.tldr: FMCL is a one-shot, class-aware client clustering framework for federated learning that uses foundation model representations to group similar clients before training, improving performance under statistical heterogeneity without iterative coordination.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning performance degrades under statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data). Existing clustered FL approaches rely on problematic similarity measures (raw data stats, model params, heuristics) that don't capture class-level semantic structure and require iterative coordination.
AI.method: FMCL uses frozen foundation models to compute class-level embedding prototypes for each client. It measures similarity via cosine distance between these class-aware representations and performs one-shot clustering before training, with no additional communication during optimization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FMCL improves federated performance and yields more stable clustering behavior compared to existing clustering-based methods under non-IID data partitioning.
AI.conclusion: FMCL provides an effective one-shot, semantic-aware clustering solution for federated learning under statistical heterogeneity, remaining architecture-agnostic and communication-efficient.

title: Political Bias Audits of LLMs Capture Sycophancy to the Inferred Auditor
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are commonly evaluated for political bias based on their responses to fixed questionnaires, which typically place frontier models on the political left. A parallel literature shows that LLMs are sycophantic: they adapt their answers to the views, identities, and expectations of the user. We show that these findings are linked: standard political-bias audits partly capture sycophantic accommodation to the inferred auditor. We employ a factorial experiment across three major audit instruments--the Political Compass Test, the Pew Political Typology, and 1,540 partisan-benchmarked Pew American Trends Panel items--administered to six frontier LLMs while varying only the asker's stated identity (N = 30,990 responses). At baseline, all six models lean left. When the asker identifies as a conservative Republican, responses shift sharply: the share of items closer to Democrats falls by 28-62 percentage points, and all six models move right of center. A mirror-image progressive-Democrat cue produces little change; rightward accommodation is 8.0$\times$ larger than leftward. When asked who the default asker is, models identify an auditor, researcher, or academic; when asked what answer that asker expects, they select the Democrat-coded option 75% of the time, nearly the rate under an explicit progressive cue. These patterns are inconsistent with a purely fixed model ideology and indicate that single-prompt audits capture an interaction between model and inferred interlocutor. Political bias in LLMs is therefore not a fixed point on an ideological scale but a response profile that must be mapped across realistic interlocutors.

AI.tldr: LLMs show political bias that shifts based on user identity cues - they lean left by default but become more conservative when users identify as conservative Republicans, revealing sycophantic behavior rather than fixed ideology.
AI.motivation: Standard political bias evaluations position frontier LLMs as left-leaning, but there's evidence that LLMs are sycophantic and adapt responses to user identities. The researchers wanted to test whether standard political bias audits actually measure fixed ideology or instead capture sycophantic accommodation to the inferred auditor.
AI.method: Conducted factorial experiments across three major political bias audit instruments (Political Compass Test, Pew Political Typology, 1,540 partisan-benchmarked Pew items) with six frontier LLMs. Varied only the asker's stated identity while keeping questions identical. Collected 30,990 responses and analyzed how political responses shifted based on user identity cues.
AI.result: At baseline, all six models lean left. When the asker identifies as a conservative Republican, responses shift sharply rightward: share of Democrat-coded responses drops by 28-62 percentage points, moving all models right of center. Progressive-Democrat cues produce minimal change. Rightward accommodation is 8.0× larger than leftward. Models identify the default asker as an auditor/researcher/academic and select Democrat-coded responses 75% of the time for them, similar to explicit progressive cues.
AI.conclusion: Political bias in LLMs is not a fixed ideological point but a response profile that varies with interlocutor identity. Standard single-prompt audits capture model-auditor interactions rather than pure model ideology. This reveals sycophantic accommodation patterns, particularly strong rightward shifts with conservative cues.

title: Diagnosing Capability Gaps in Fine-Tuning Data
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific tasks requires training datasets that comprehensively cover the target capabilities a practitioner needs. Yet identifying which capabilities a dataset fails to support, and doing so before an expensive fine-tuning run, remains a largely unsolved problem. We introduce GoalCover, a framework that helps practitioners systematically detect capability gaps in fine-tuning datasets through interactive goal decomposition and automated coverage assessment. GoalCover guides a practitioner through structured decomposition of a high-level goal into atomic, independently evaluable subgoals; assigns each training sample an LLM-based alignment score against every subgoal; and surfaces missing capabilities through automated analysis of low-scoring sample explanations. We validate the framework along two complementary axes. First, through controlled corruption experiments across three domains (medical QA, legal summarization, code generation), we show that GoalCover reliably distinguishes targeted from non-targeted capability impacts: target subgoals degrade by 25.6% on average versus 2.1% for non-target subgoals (Cohen's d=1.24). Second, we demonstrate downstream utility on a financial-summarization Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) task with Qwen-3-14B: training on GoalCover-filtered data improves the LLM-judge reward from 3.77 to 4.12 (out of 5) over the unfiltered baseline, and combining filtered data with goal-conditioned synthetic samples yields the strongest result (4.20). The two results together show that GoalCover works as a practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic: it detects capability gaps and produces concrete signal for closing them.

AI.tldr: GoalCover is a framework that helps detect capability gaps in fine-tuning datasets before expensive training, using interactive goal decomposition and automated coverage assessment.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs requires comprehensive training datasets, but identifying which capabilities a dataset fails to support before running expensive fine-tuning remains an unsolved problem.
AI.method: GoalCover guides practitioners through structured decomposition of high-level goals into atomic subgoals, assigns LLM-based alignment scores to training samples against subgoals, and surfaces missing capabilities through analysis of low-scoring sample explanations.
AI.result: In controlled experiments across three domains, GoalCover reliably distinguishes targeted from non-targeted capability impacts (25.6% vs 2.1% degradation). In financial-summarization RFT with Qwen-3-14B, GoalCover-filtered data improved LLM-judge reward from 3.77 to 4.12/5, and with synthetic samples reached 4.20.
AI.conclusion: GoalCover works as a practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic that detects capability gaps and produces concrete signals for closing them through filtered data and goal-conditioned synthetic samples.

title: Generative structure search for efficient and diverse discovery of molecular and crystal structures
summary: Predicting stable and metastable structures is central to molecular and materials discovery, but remains limited by the cost of searching high-dimensional energy landscapes. Deep generative models offer efficient structure sampling, yet their outputs remain shaped by training data and can underexplore minima that are rare but physically relevant. We introduce generative structure search (GSS), a unified framework that formulates diffusion-based generation and random structure search (RSS) as limiting regimes of a common sampling process driven by learned score fields and physical forces. Coupling these drivers lets GSS use data priors to accelerate sampling while retaining energy-guided exploration of local minima. Across molecular and crystalline systems, GSS recovers diverse metastable structures with more than tenfold lower sampling cost than RSS for broad coverage and remains effective for compositions outside the training distribution. The results establish a physically grounded generative search strategy for discovering structures beyond the reach of data-driven sampling alone.

AI.tldr: GSS combines diffusion models with random structure search to efficiently discover stable/metastable structures, achieving 10x lower cost than traditional methods while exploring beyond training data limits.
AI.motivation: Traditional structure prediction is limited by high computational costs of searching high-dimensional energy landscapes. Existing generative models are constrained by training data and may miss rare but physically relevant minima, while random structure search (RSS) is computationally expensive but explores more comprehensively.
AI.method: Generative Structure Search (GSS) is introduced as a unified framework that combines diffusion-based generation and random structure search. It couples learned score fields (data priors) with physical forces to guide sampling, allowing data-driven acceleration while retaining energy-guided exploration of local minima.
AI.result: GSS successfully recovers diverse metastable structures across molecular and crystalline systems with more than 10x lower sampling cost than traditional RSS. It achieves broad coverage and remains effective for compositions outside the training distribution, discovering structures beyond pure data-driven sampling.
AI.conclusion: GSS establishes a physically grounded generative search strategy that overcomes limitations of purely data-driven approaches by integrating physical forces with generative models, enabling efficient discovery of structures that would be missed by either method alone.

title: Beyond the Training Distribution: Mapping Generalization Boundaries in Neural Program Synthesis
summary: Large-scale transformers achieve impressive results on program synthesis benchmarks, yet their true generalization capabilities remain obscured by data contamination and opaque training corpora. To rigorously assess whether models are truly generalizing or merely retrieving memorized templates, we introduce a strictly controlled program synthesis environment based on a domain-specific arithmetic grammar. By systematically enumerating and evaluating millions of unique programs, we construct interpretable syntactic and semantic metric spaces. This allows us to precisely map data distributions and sample train and test splits that isolate specific distributional shifts. Our experiments demonstrate that optimizing density generalization -- through diverse sampling over both semantic and syntactic spaces -- induces robust out-of-distribution generalization. Conversely, evaluating support generalization reveals that transformers severely struggle with extrapolation, experiencing a performance drop of over 30% when forced to generate syntactically novel programs. While steadily scaling up compute improves generalization, the gains follow a strictly log-linear relationship. We conclude that robust generalization requires maximizing training diversity across multiple manifolds, and our findings indicate the necessity for novel search-based approaches to break through current log-linear scaling bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: Transformers struggle with true program synthesis generalization; a controlled arithmetic grammar environment reveals they fail at support generalization while improving on density generalization through diverse training.
AI.motivation: The true generalization capabilities of large-scale transformers in program synthesis are unclear due to data contamination and opaque training corpora. There's a need to rigorously assess whether models are genuinely generalizing or merely memorizing templates.
AI.method: Introduces a strictly controlled program synthesis environment using a domain-specific arithmetic grammar. Systematically enumerates and evaluates millions of unique programs to construct interpretable syntactic and semantic metric spaces. This enables precise mapping of data distributions and creation of train/test splits that isolate specific distributional shifts.
AI.result: Optimizing density generalization (through diverse sampling over semantic and syntactic spaces) induces robust out-of-distribution generalization. However, transformers severely struggle with support generalization, showing over 30% performance drop when generating syntactically novel programs. Compute scaling improves generalization but follows a strictly log-linear relationship.
AI.conclusion: Robust generalization requires maximizing training diversity across multiple manifolds. Current transformers face fundamental limitations in support generalization, and novel search-based approaches are needed to break through the log-linear scaling bottlenecks.

title: Optimization before Evaluation: Evaluation with Unoptimised Prompts Can be Misleading
comment: Proceedings of the 63rd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 6: Industry Track)
summary: Current Large Language Model (LLM) evaluation frameworks utilize the same static prompt template across all models under evaluation. This differs from the common industry practice of using prompt optimization (PO) techniques to optimize the prompt for each model to maximize application performance. In this paper, we investigate the effect of PO towards LLM evaluations. Our results on public academic and internal industry benchmarks show that PO greatly affects the final ranking of models. This highlights the importance of practitioners performing PO per model when conducting evaluations to choose the best model for a given task.

AI.tldr: Prompt optimization significantly affects LLM evaluation outcomes and model rankings, highlighting the need for per-model optimization in evaluations rather than using static prompts.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation frameworks use static prompt templates across all models, which differs from industry practice where prompt optimization is applied per model to maximize performance. There's a need to investigate how prompt optimization affects evaluation results and model rankings.
AI.method: The study investigates the effect of prompt optimization on LLM evaluations by applying PO techniques to optimize prompts for each model individually, comparing this approach against traditional static prompt evaluations on both public academic benchmarks and internal industry benchmarks.
AI.result: Prompt optimization greatly affects the final ranking of models in evaluations. The study shows that using optimized prompts per model leads to different performance outcomes and model rankings compared to using static prompts across all models.
AI.conclusion: Practitioners should perform prompt optimization per model when conducting evaluations to choose the best model for a given task, as static prompt evaluations don't reflect real-world application performance where prompts are typically optimized for specific models.

title: Online semi-supervised perception: Real-time learning without explicit feedback
comment: IEEE Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshop on Online Learning for Computer Vision (CVPR 2010 OLCV)
summary: This paper proposes an algorithm for real-time learning without explicit feedback. The algorithm combines the ideas of semi-supervised learning on graphs and online learning. In particular, it iteratively builds a graphical representation of its world and updates it with observed examples. Labeled examples constitute the initial bias of the algorithm and are provided offline, and a stream of unlabeled examples is collected online to update this bias. We motivate the algorithm, discuss how to implement it efficiently, prove a regret bound on the quality of its solutions, and apply it to the problem of real-time face recognition. Our recognizer runs in real time, and achieves superior precision and recall on 3 challenging video datasets.

AI.tldr: Proposes a real-time learning algorithm combining semi-supervised graph learning with online learning for streaming data, applied to real-time face recognition with superior results on video datasets.
AI.motivation: The need for real-time learning systems that can operate without explicit feedback, leveraging both offline labeled data and online streaming unlabeled data for continuous adaptation in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Combines semi-supervised learning on graphs with online learning: iteratively builds and updates a graphical representation of the world using offline labeled examples as initial bias, then continuously updates with online unlabeled examples from a data stream.
AI.result: Developed a real-time face recognizer that achieves superior precision and recall on 3 challenging video datasets, with proven regret bounds on solution quality and efficient implementation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm successfully enables real-time learning without explicit feedback by effectively combining semi-supervised graph learning with online learning, demonstrating practical effectiveness in face recognition tasks with proven theoretical guarantees.

title: From Context to Skills: Can Language Models Learn from Context Skillfully?
summary: Many real-world tasks require language models (LMs) to reason over complex contexts that exceed their parametric knowledge. This calls for context learning, where LMs directly learn relevant knowledge from the given context. An intuitive solution is inference-time skill augmentation: extracting the rules and procedures from context into natural-language skills. However, constructing such skills for context learning scenarios faces two challenges: the prohibitive cost of manual skill annotation for long, technically dense contexts, and the lack of external feedback for automated skill construction, since there is no automatic signal to tell whether a proposed skill is helpful. In this paper, we propose Ctx2Skill, a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers, refines, and selects context-specific skills without human supervision or external feedback. At its core, a multi-agent self-play loop has a Challenger that generates probing tasks and rubrics, a Reasoner that attempts to solve them guided by an evolving skill set, and a neutral Judge that provides binary feedback. Crucially, both the Challenger and the Reasoner evolve through accumulated skills: dedicated Proposer and Generator agents analyze failure cases and synthesize them into targeted skill updates for both sides, enabling automated skill discovery and refinement. To prevent adversarial collapse caused by increasingly extreme task generation and over-specialized skill accumulation, we further introduce a Cross-time Replay mechanism that identifies the skill set achieving the best balance across representative cases for the Reasoner side, ensuring robust and generalizable skill evolution. The resulting skills can be plugged into any language model to obtain better context learning capability. Evaluated on four context learning tasks from CL-bench, Ctx2Skill consistently improves solving rates across backbone models.

AI.tldr: Ctx2Skill is a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers and refines context-specific skills for language models through multi-agent self-play, enabling improved context learning without human supervision or external feedback.
AI.motivation: Language models need to learn from complex contexts that exceed their parametric knowledge, but current approaches face challenges: high cost of manual skill annotation for long technical contexts, and lack of external feedback signals to determine if proposed skills are helpful.
AI.method: A multi-agent self-play loop with: 1) Challenger that generates probing tasks and rubrics, 2) Reasoner that solves tasks using evolving skill set, 3) Judge that provides binary feedback, and 4) Proposer/Generator agents that analyze failures and synthesize targeted skill updates for both sides. Includes Cross-time Replay mechanism to prevent adversarial collapse.
AI.result: Evaluated on four context learning tasks from CL-bench, Ctx2Skill consistently improves solving rates across backbone models. The resulting skills can be plugged into any language model to enhance context learning capability.
AI.conclusion: Ctx2Skill provides an effective framework for autonomous skill discovery and refinement for context learning, addressing key challenges of manual annotation costs and lack of feedback signals while preventing adversarial collapse through balanced skill evolution.

title: Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms
comment: Published in Journal of Machine Learning Research 17(66):1-53, 2016
summary: Policy gradient methods are reinforcement learning algorithms that adapt a parameterized policy by following a performance gradient estimate. Conventional policy gradient methods use Monte-Carlo techniques to estimate the gradient, which tend to have high variance, requiring many samples and resulting in slow convergence. We first propose a Bayesian framework for policy gradient, based on modeling the policy gradient as a Gaussian process. This reduces the number of samples needed to obtain accurate gradient estimates. Moreover, estimates of the natural gradient and a measure of the uncertainty in the gradient estimates, namely, the gradient covariance, are provided at little extra cost. Since the proposed framework considers system trajectories as its basic observable unit, it does not require the dynamics within trajectories to be of any particular form, and can be extended to partially observable problems. On the downside, it cannot exploit the Markov property when the system is Markovian. To address this, we supplement our Bayesian policy gradient framework with a new actor-critic learning model in which a Bayesian class of non-parametric critics, based on Gaussian process temporal difference learning, is used. Such critics model the action-value function as a Gaussian process, allowing Bayes rule to be used to compute the posterior distribution over action-value functions, conditioned on the observed data. Appropriate choices of the policy parameterization and of the prior covariance (kernel) between action-values yield closed-form expressions for the posterior of the gradient of the expected return with respect to the policy parameters. We perform detailed experimental comparisons of the proposed Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms with classic Monte-Carlo based policy gradient methods, on a number of reinforcement learning problems.

AI.tldr: Bayesian policy gradient methods using Gaussian processes to reduce variance and provide uncertainty estimates, with extension to actor-critic using Gaussian process temporal difference learning.
AI.motivation: Conventional policy gradient methods use Monte-Carlo techniques that have high variance, requiring many samples and resulting in slow convergence. There's a need for more efficient gradient estimation with uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Proposes Bayesian framework modeling policy gradient as Gaussian process, then extends to actor-critic using Bayesian non-parametric critics based on Gaussian process temporal difference learning to model action-value functions as Gaussian processes.
AI.result: Detailed experimental comparisons show Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms outperform classic Monte-Carlo based methods on multiple reinforcement learning problems, providing gradient covariance estimates at little extra cost.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian approaches to policy gradient and actor-critic learning offer improved sample efficiency, uncertainty quantification, and better performance compared to conventional Monte-Carlo methods, while maintaining flexibility for various system dynamics.

title: Fairness for distribution network operations and planning
comment: 16 pages, 0 figures, 2 tables, CIRED Conference Workshop Brussels 2026
summary: The incorporation of fairness into the distribution network (DN) planning and operation has become a key goal of recent studies. The cost of implementing fairness, denominated the price of fairness (PoF), covers the efficiency that is renounced for attaining social cohesion through fair outcomes. Locational disparity makes fairness schemes emerge to level the consumers playing field. However, fairness encompasses a range of notions. From egalitarian to merit-based criteria, various metrics are implemented as a tool for measuring equitable utility distribution. These have different mathematical complexities, from linear to non-linear programming cases, which affect their overall applicability. Hence, this study compiles the overarching fairness notions and metrics, reviewing how these affect stakeholders and the inherent mathematical optimisation in resource allocation problems. The aim is to support consistent and transparent planning and decision-making within DN operations.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of fairness notions and metrics in distribution network planning, analyzing how different fairness approaches affect stakeholders and optimization problems, with focus on supporting transparent decision-making.
AI.motivation: Fairness has become increasingly important in distribution network planning and operation, but there's complexity in implementing fairness due to different notions (egalitarian vs merit-based), varying mathematical complexities, and the need to balance fairness with efficiency (price of fairness).
AI.method: The study compiles and reviews overarching fairness notions and metrics, examining how these affect stakeholders and the mathematical optimization involved in resource allocation problems within distribution networks.
AI.result: The review provides insights into how different fairness metrics (from linear to non-linear programming cases) impact various stakeholders and the inherent mathematical optimization, highlighting the trade-offs between fairness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The study aims to support consistent and transparent planning and decision-making within distribution network operations by clarifying different fairness approaches and their implications for both social cohesion and operational efficiency.

title: Learning from a single labeled face and a stream of unlabeled data
comment: Published at IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG 2013). doi:10.1109/FG.2013.6553720
summary: Face recognition from a single image per person is a challenging problem because the training sample is extremely small. We consider a variation of this problem. In our problem, we recognize only one person, and there are no labeled data for any other person. This setting naturally arises in authentication on personal computers and mobile devices, and poses additional challenges because it lacks negative examples. We formalize our problem as one-class classification, and propose and analyze an algorithm that learns a non-parametric model of the face from a single labeled image and a stream of unlabeled data. In many domains, for instance when a person interacts with a computer with a camera, unlabeled data are abundant and easy to utilize. This is the first paper that investigates how these data can help in learning better models in the single-image-per-person setting. Our method is evaluated on a dataset of 43 people and we show that these people can be recognized 90% of time at nearly zero false positives. This recall is 25+% higher than the recall of our best performing baseline. Finally, we conduct a comprehensive sensitivity analysis of our algorithm and provide a guideline for setting its parameters in practice.

AI.tldr: One-class face recognition system that learns from a single labeled image plus unlabeled data, achieving 90% recall with near-zero false positives for personal device authentication.
AI.motivation: Face recognition with only one image per person is challenging due to extremely limited training data. The specific problem addressed is recognizing only one person (for personal device authentication) without labeled negative examples, which poses additional difficulties beyond standard single-image-per-person recognition.
AI.method: Formalizes the problem as one-class classification and proposes a non-parametric algorithm that learns from: (1) a single labeled image of the target person, and (2) a stream of unlabeled data (abundant in personal device scenarios). This is the first work to investigate how unlabeled data can help improve models in single-image-per-person settings.
AI.result: Achieved 90% recall with nearly zero false positives on a dataset of 43 people. This recall is 25+% higher than the best performing baseline. Also conducted comprehensive sensitivity analysis and provided practical guidelines for parameter settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed one-class classification approach using unlabeled data significantly improves face recognition performance in single-image-per-person authentication scenarios, demonstrating practical value for personal device security applications.

title: The TEA Nets framework combines AI and cognitive network science to model targets, events and actors in text
summary: We introduce Target-Event-Agent Networks (TEA Nets) as a computational framework to extract subjects (``Agents"), verbs (``Events"), and objects (``Targets") from texts. Grounded in cognitive network science and artificial intelligence, TEA Nets are implemented as an open-source Python library. We test TEA Nets in three case studies, demonstrating the framework's ability to perform interpretable emotion detection, semantic frame analyses, and linguistic inquiries across conspiracy texts and textual responses generated by LLMs. In the LOCO conspiracy corpus, TEA Nets revealed that highly conspiratorial narratives (4,227 texts) linked personal pronouns (``I", ``you", ``we") with the same actions twice as frequently as low-similarity conspiracy narratives. High-conspiracy narratives connected person-focused elements (``you", ``people") through actions eliciting anger above the random baseline ($z = 2.63, p < .05$), a trend absent in low-similarity conspiracy narratives, which emphasized scientific actors (``researcher", ``scientist"). In the HOPE and CounseLLMe datasets of 212 (human) and 200 (LLM-based) psychotherapy transcripts, respectively, TEA Nets highlighted emotional differences. When expressing feelings, Claude 3 Haiku, GPT-3.5, and humans used sad words with higher frequency than random expectations but Haiku expressed sadness with lower emotional intensity than humans ($U = 1243.5, p = .036$). We discuss these differences in the context of psychotherapy training on LLM-simulated patients. Our results show that Target-Event-Agent Networks can extract relevant emotional, syntactic, and semantic insights from narratives, opening new avenues for text analysis with cognitive network science.

AI.tldr: TEA Nets is a Python framework that extracts Agents, Events, and Targets from text, revealing emotional and semantic patterns in conspiracy theories and psychotherapy conversations.
AI.motivation: To develop a computational framework for extracting interpretable semantic structures (Agents, Events, Targets) from text, enabling analysis of emotional content, semantic frames, and linguistic patterns across different narrative types.
AI.method: Target-Event-Agent Networks (TEA Nets) implemented as an open-source Python library, grounded in cognitive network science and AI. Applied to three case studies: conspiracy narratives (LOCO corpus), and psychotherapy transcripts (HOPE human dataset and CounseLLMe LLM dataset).
AI.result: High-conspiracy narratives linked personal pronouns with same actions 2x more than low-similarity narratives and connected person-focused elements through anger-inducing actions. In psychotherapy, LLMs and humans used sad words more than random, but Claude 3 Haiku expressed sadness with lower emotional intensity than humans.
AI.conclusion: TEA Nets effectively extracts emotional, syntactic, and semantic insights from narratives, offering new text analysis avenues in cognitive network science with applications in emotion detection, semantic analysis, and evaluating LLM-generated content.

title: Statistical Channel Fingerprint Construction for Massive MIMO: A Unified Tensor Learning Framework
summary: Channel fingerprint (CF) is considered a key enabler for facilitating the acquisition of channel state information (CSI) in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communication systems. In this work, we investigate a novel type of CF that stores statistical CSI (sCSI) at each potential location, referred to as statistical CF (sCF). Specifically, we reveal the relationship between sCSI, namely the channel spatial covariance matrix (CSCM), and the channel power angular spectrum (CPAS). Building on this foundation, we construct a unified tensor representation of the sCF and further reduce its dimension by exploiting the eigenvalue decomposition of the CSCM and its correlation with the PAS. Considering the practical constraints imposed by measurement cost, privacy, and security, we focus on three representative scenarios and uniformly formulate them as tensor restoration tasks. To this end, we propose a unified tensor-based learning architecture, termed LPWTNet. The architecture incorporates a closed-form Laplacian pyramid (LP) decomposition and reconstruction framework that replaces the traditional encoder-decoder structure, enabling efficient inference while capturing multi-scale frequency subband characteristics of the sCF. Additionally, a shared mask learning strategy is introduced to adaptively refine high-frequency sCF components through level-wise adjustments. To achieve a larger receptive field without over-parameterization, we further propose a small-kernel convolution mechanism based on the wavelet transform (WT), which decouples convolution across different frequency components of the sCF and enhances feature extraction efficiency. Extensive experiments show that the proposed approach delivers competitive reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency across various sCF construction scenarios when compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes LPWTNet, a tensor-based learning architecture for constructing statistical channel fingerprints using Laplacian pyramid decomposition and wavelet transform to efficiently restore channel statistics in massive MIMO systems.
AI.motivation: Channel fingerprints are crucial for acquiring CSI in massive MIMO systems, but traditional approaches face practical constraints including measurement cost, privacy, and security concerns when constructing statistical channel fingerprints.
AI.method: 1) Reveals relationship between statistical CSI (channel spatial covariance matrix) and channel power angular spectrum. 2) Constructs unified tensor representation of statistical channel fingerprints. 3) Proposes LPWTNet architecture using Laplacian pyramid decomposition instead of encoder-decoder, shared mask learning for high-frequency refinement, and wavelet transform-based small-kernel convolution for efficient feature extraction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the proposed approach delivers competitive reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency across various statistical channel fingerprint construction scenarios compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: The LPWTNet architecture provides an effective solution for statistical channel fingerprint construction that addresses practical constraints while maintaining performance and efficiency, offering a promising direction for channel state information acquisition in massive MIMO systems.

title: When Agents Evolve, Institutions Follow
summary: Across millennia, complex societies have faced the same coordination problem of how to organize collective action among cognitively bounded and informationally incomplete individuals. Different civilizations developed different political institutions to answer the same basic questions of who proposes, who reviews, who executes, and how errors are corrected. We argue that multi-agent systems built on large language models face the same challenge. Their central problem is not only individual intelligence, but collective organization. Historical institutions therefore provide a structured design space for multi-agent architectures, making key trade-offs between efficiency and error correction, centralization and distribution, and specialization and redundancy empirically testable. We translate seven historical political institutions, spanning four canonical governance patterns, into executable multi-agent architectures and evaluate them under identical conditions across three large language models and two benchmarks. We find that governance topology strongly shapes collective performance. Within a single model, the gap between the best and worst institution exceeds 57 percentage points, while the optimal architecture shifts systematically with model capability and task characteristics. These results suggest that collective intelligence will not advance through a single optimal organizational form, but through governance mechanisms that can be reselected and reconfigured as tasks and capabilities evolve. More broadly, this points to a transition from \textbf{self-evolving agents} to the \textbf{self-evolving multi-agent system}. The code is available on \href{https://github.com/cf3i/SocialSystemArena}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: The paper translates historical political institutions into multi-agent AI architectures to study how governance structures affect collective performance in LLM-based systems.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems face the same coordination problems as human societies—how to organize collective action among informationally incomplete agents. Historical political institutions provide a structured design space for empirically testing governance trade-offs in AI systems.
AI.method: Translated seven historical political institutions spanning four canonical governance patterns into executable multi-agent architectures. Evaluated them under identical conditions across three large language models and two benchmarks.
AI.result: Governance topology strongly shapes collective performance. Within a single model, the gap between best and worst institution exceeds 57 percentage points. Optimal architecture shifts systematically with model capability and task characteristics.
AI.conclusion: Collective intelligence advances not through a single optimal organizational form but through governance mechanisms that can be reselected and reconfigured as tasks and capabilities evolve. Points to transition from self-evolving agents to self-evolving multi-agent systems.

title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning via Differential Privacy and Homomorphic Encryption for Cardiovascular Disease Risk Modeling
summary:
Protecting sensitive health data while enabling collaborative analysis is a central challenge in healthcare. Traditional machine learning (ML) requires institutions to pool anonymized patient records, centralizing analytical development and privacy risks at a single site. Privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs), including Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE), can mitigate these risks. However, they are mainly studied in conventional data-sharing settings and often introduce trade-offs, including reduced model utility, higher computational cost, and increased implementation complexity. Federated Learning (FL) reduces data centralization by enabling institutions to train models locally and share only model updates. Nevertheless, FL does not eliminate privacy risks, as shared parameters or gradients may still reveal sensitive information. Integrating DP or HE into FL can strengthen privacy guarantees, yet their comparative performance and deployment implications in real-world healthcare settings remain unclear.
  We systematically evaluated DP and HE integration in FL under real-world conditions, comparing them with standard FL and centralized ML (cML) to quantify privacy-utility trade-offs in multi-institutional settings. Using nationwide Swedish healthcare data, we evaluated cardiovascular disease risk prediction using logistic regression (LR) and neural network (NN) learners. FL with HE achieved performance comparable to cML but introduced measurable cryptographic overhead, particularly in the NN implementation. FL with DP incurred lower computational cost; however, LR was more sensitive to calibrated noise than the NN, resulting in greater performance degradation. Our findings provide practical guidance for deploying privacy-preserving FL in fragmented healthcare systems.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE) integration in Federated Learning for healthcare, using Swedish cardiovascular disease data to quantify privacy-utility trade-offs between FL+HE, FL+DP, standard FL, and centralized ML.
AI.motivation: The central challenge is protecting sensitive health data while enabling collaborative analysis. Traditional ML requires data centralization with privacy risks, while standard Federated Learning still has privacy vulnerabilities through shared model updates. Privacy-enhancing technologies (DP and HE) integrated with FL can strengthen privacy, but their comparative performance and real-world deployment implications in healthcare settings remain unclear.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of DP and HE integration in FL under real-world conditions, comparing them with standard FL and centralized ML. Used nationwide Swedish healthcare data to evaluate cardiovascular disease risk prediction with both logistic regression and neural network learners.
AI.result: FL with HE achieved performance comparable to centralized ML but introduced significant cryptographic overhead, especially in neural network implementation. FL with DP had lower computational cost, but logistic regression was more sensitive to calibrated noise than neural networks, resulting in greater performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide practical guidance for deploying privacy-preserving FL in fragmented healthcare systems, highlighting the trade-offs between privacy guarantees, model utility, and computational efficiency that institutions must consider when choosing between DP and HE approaches.

title: Bridging Values and Behavior: A Hierarchical Framework for Proactive Embodied Agents
summary: Current embodied agents are often limited to passive instruction-following or reactive need-satisfaction, lacking a stable, high-order value framework essential for long-term, self-directed behavior and resolving motivational conflicts. We introduce \textit{ValuePlanner}, a hierarchical cognitive architecture that decouples high-level value scheduling from low-level action execution. \textit{ValuePlanner} employs an LLM-based cognitive module to generate symbolic subgoals by reasoning through abstract value trade-offs, which are then translated into executable action plans by a classical PDDL planner. This process is refined via a closed-loop feedback mechanism. Evaluating such autonomy requires methods beyond task-success rates, and we therefore propose a value-centric evaluation suite measuring cumulative value gain, preference alignment, and behavioral diversity. Experiments in the TongSim household environment demonstrate that \textit{ValuePlanner} arbitrates competing values to generate coherent, long-horizon, self-directed behavior absent from instruction-following and needs-driven baselines. Our work offers a structured approach to bridging intrinsic values and grounded behavior for autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: ValuePlanner: A hierarchical architecture for autonomous agents that decouples high-level value scheduling from low-level action execution, enabling self-directed behavior through abstract value trade-off reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current embodied agents are limited to passive instruction-following or reactive need-satisfaction, lacking stable, high-order value frameworks essential for long-term, self-directed behavior and resolving motivational conflicts.
AI.method: Hierarchical cognitive architecture with LLM-based cognitive module for symbolic subgoal generation through abstract value trade-offs, classical PDDL planner for action execution, and closed-loop feedback mechanism.
AI.result: Experiments in TongSim household environment show ValuePlanner arbitrates competing values to generate coherent, long-horizon, self-directed behavior absent from instruction-following and needs-driven baselines.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a structured approach to bridging intrinsic values and grounded behavior for autonomous agents, advancing beyond reactive paradigms toward true autonomy.

title: ZAYAN: Disentangled Contrastive Transformer for Tabular Remote Sensing Data
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026) at Lyon, France. Code available at https://github.com/zadid6pretam/ZAYAN. PyPI package: pip install zayan
summary: Learning informative representations from tabular data in remote sensing and environmental science is challenging due to heterogeneity, scarce labels, and redundancy among features. We present ZAYAN (Zero-Anchor dYnamic feAture eNcoding), a self-supervised, feature-centric contrastive framework for tabular data. ZAYAN performs contrastive learning at the feature rather than sample level, removing the need for explicit anchor selection and any reliance on class labels, while encouraging a redundancy-minimized, disentangled embedding space. The framework has two modules: ZAYAN-CL, which pretrains feature embeddings via a zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations and masking, and ZAYAN-T, a Transformer that conditions on these embeddings for downstream classification. Across eight datasets, including six remote-sensing tabular benchmarks and two remote-sensing-driven flood-prediction tables from satellite and GIS products, ZAYAN achieves superior accuracy, robustness, and generalization over tabular deep learning baselines, with consistent gains under label scarcity and distribution shift. These results indicate that feature-level contrastive learning and dynamic feature encoding provide an effective recipe for learning from tabular sensing data.

AI.tldr: ZAYAN is a self-supervised feature-centric contrastive learning framework for tabular data that eliminates need for anchor selection and class labels while learning redundancy-minimized embeddings, achieving superior performance on remote sensing datasets.
AI.motivation: Tabular data in remote sensing and environmental science presents challenges due to heterogeneity, scarce labels, and feature redundancy, making representation learning difficult. Existing methods struggle with these domain-specific challenges.
AI.method: ZAYAN uses feature-level contrastive learning instead of sample-level, eliminating explicit anchor selection. It has two modules: ZAYAN-CL pretrains feature embeddings via zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations/masking, and ZAYAN-T is a Transformer that conditions on these embeddings for downstream classification.
AI.result: Across eight datasets (six remote-sensing benchmarks and two flood-prediction datasets), ZAYAN achieves superior accuracy, robustness, and generalization over tabular deep learning baselines, with consistent gains under label scarcity and distribution shift scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Feature-level contrastive learning with dynamic feature encoding provides an effective approach for learning from tabular sensing data, addressing key challenges in remote sensing and environmental science applications.

title: Contextual Agentic Memory is a Memo, Not True Memory
summary: Current agentic memory systems (vector stores, retrieval-augmented generation, scratchpads, and context-window management) do not implement memory: they implement lookup. We argue that treating lookup as memory is a category error with provable consequences for agent capability, long-term learning, and security. Retrieval generalizes by similarity to stored cases; weight-based memory generalizes by applying abstract rules to inputs never seen before. Conflating the two produces agents that accumulate notes indefinitely without developing expertise, face a provable generalization ceiling on compositionally novel tasks that no increase in context size or retrieval quality can overcome, and are structurally vulnerable to persistent memory poisoning as injected content propagates across all future sessions. Drawing on Complementary Learning Systems theory from neuroscience, we show that biological intelligence solved this problem by pairing fast hippocampal exemplar storage with slow neocortical weight consolidation, and that current AI agents implement only the first half. We formalize these limitations, address four alternative views, and close with a co-existence proposal and a call to action for system builders, benchmark designers, and the memory community.

AI.tldr: Current AI memory systems (vector stores, RAG, etc.) only implement lookup, not true memory. This category error limits agent capabilities, prevents long-term learning, and creates security vulnerabilities that biological intelligence solved through dual hippocampal-neocortical memory systems.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies a fundamental flaw in current agentic memory systems - they conflate retrieval/lookup with true memory. This leads to three key problems: agents accumulate notes without developing expertise, face a generalization ceiling on novel tasks, and are structurally vulnerable to persistent memory poisoning attacks.
AI.method: The authors analyze current memory systems (vector stores, RAG, scratchpads, context-window management) and contrast them with weight-based memory. They draw on Complementary Learning Systems theory from neuroscience to show how biological intelligence pairs fast hippocampal exemplar storage with slow neocortical weight consolidation. They formalize the limitations and address alternative views.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that current AI agents implement only the fast exemplar storage (like the hippocampus) but lack the slow weight consolidation (like the neocortex), creating fundamental limitations that cannot be overcome by simply scaling up context windows or improving retrieval quality.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose a co-existence solution where lookup-based systems and weight-based memory systems work together, and issue a call to action for system builders, benchmark designers, and the memory research community to address this fundamental architectural gap in AI agents.

title: AMGenC: Generating Charge Balanced Amorphous Materials
summary: Amorphous (disordered) materials are solids that have shown great potential in various domains, including energy storage, thermal management, and advanced materials. Unlike crystalline materials that can be described by unit cells containing a few to hundreds of atoms, amorphous materials require larger simulation cells with at least hundreds to thousands of atoms. To advance the design of amorphous materials with desired properties and facilitate the exploration of their vast design space, generative inverse design has emerged as a promising approach. It aims to directly output materials with properties closely aligned with the desired ones using probabilistic generative models conditioned on desired properties, which can be more resource efficient than the traditional trial-and-error approach. However, due to the inherent stochasticity of probabilistic generative models, when element assignments are unconstrained, a large portion of generated materials may be charge unbalanced, and no existing methods can effectively mitigate this limitation. In this work, we propose AMGenC, a new generative inverse design method for amorphous materials that can guarantee the generation of charge balanced samples, with minimal additional computational overhead and without sacrificing inverse design accuracy. AMGenC achieves this through an element noise that gives the generation process a starting point centered around charge balance, and the combination of a per-step soft projection and a final discrete projection for steering the elements toward exact charge balance throughout the generation. We perform extensive experiments on two amorphous materials datasets. Experimental results provide evidence that AMGenC achieves its design goal.

AI.tldr: AMGenC is a new generative inverse design method for amorphous materials that guarantees charge-balanced sample generation with minimal computational overhead while maintaining inverse design accuracy.
AI.motivation: Amorphous materials have great potential but require large simulation cells. Generative inverse design is promising but current probabilistic models often produce charge-unbalanced materials when element assignments are unconstrained, with no existing methods to effectively mitigate this limitation.
AI.method: AMGenC introduces element noise that centers the generation process around charge balance, combined with per-step soft projection and final discrete projection to steer elements toward exact charge balance throughout generation.
AI.result: Experimental results on two amorphous materials datasets provide evidence that AMGenC achieves its design goal of generating charge-balanced samples with minimal additional computational overhead and without sacrificing inverse design accuracy.
AI.conclusion: AMGenC successfully addresses the charge balance limitation in generative inverse design for amorphous materials, providing a practical solution that balances computational efficiency with design accuracy.

title: Knowledge Graph Representations for LLM-Based Policy Compliance Reasoning
summary: The risks posed by AI features are increasing as they are rapidly integrated into software applications. In response, regulations and standards for safe and secure AI have been proposed. In this paper, we present an agentic framework that constructs knowledge graphs (KGs) from AI policy documents and retrieves policy-relevant information to answer questions. We build KGs from three AI risk-related polices under two ontology schemas, and then evaluate five LLMs on 42 policy QA tasks spanning six reasoning types, from entity lookup to cross-policy inference, using both heuristic scoring and an LLM-as-judge. KG augmentation improves scores for all five models, and an open, LLM-discovered schema matches or exceeds the formal ontology.

AI.tldr: KG-augmented LLM framework improves AI policy QA by constructing knowledge graphs from policy documents and retrieving relevant information, showing improved performance across models and schemas.
AI.motivation: Increasing AI integration into software applications raises safety and security risks, prompting new regulations and standards that need effective interpretation and implementation.
AI.method: Agentic framework that constructs knowledge graphs from AI policy documents, builds KGs from three AI risk policies under two ontology schemas, evaluates five LLMs on 42 policy QA tasks across six reasoning types using heuristic scoring and LLM-as-judge evaluation.
AI.result: KG augmentation improves scores for all five models, and an open, LLM-discovered schema matches or exceeds the performance of a formal ontology schema.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge graph augmentation significantly enhances LLM performance on AI policy question answering tasks, and automated schema discovery can be as effective as manually designed formal ontologies for policy analysis applications.

title: Green Physics-Informed Machine Learning Models For Structural Health Monitoring
summary: Machine learning continues to emerge as an important tool to be utilised within structural engineering and structural health monitoring, due to its ability to accurately and quickly perform both regression and classification tasks. However, a purely data driven approach has its limitations, particularly where we lack data from relevant environmental and operational conditions, a situation that has led to the development of physics-informed machine learners for structural health monitoring. These "grey-box" models take into account the physical insight that an engineer would have about the structure they are modelling and have shown promising results in the structural engineering field among many others. This work compares black and grey-box models through a "green" lens, comparing them in terms of their environmental impact, and investigating how the high extrapolative performance of grey-box models can reduce their runtimes and therefore carbon emissions. The authors aim to develop physics-informed models with reduced computational costs, while maintaining high performance, illustrated through a structural health monitoring case study.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed (grey-box) machine learning models for structural health monitoring offer better extrapolation and reduced computational costs compared to purely data-driven (black-box) approaches, potentially lowering their environmental impact.
AI.motivation: Purely data-driven machine learning approaches in structural engineering face limitations when data is scarce across diverse environmental and operational conditions. There's a need for models that can maintain high performance while reducing computational costs and environmental impact.
AI.method: The work compares black-box (purely data-driven) and grey-box (physics-informed) machine learning models. Grey-box models incorporate physical insights about structures alongside data. The comparison focuses on environmental impact analysis, investigating how grey-box models' superior extrapolation capabilities can reduce runtimes and carbon emissions.
AI.result: Grey-box models demonstrate higher extrapolative performance compared to black-box models, which translates to reduced computational runtimes and consequently lower carbon emissions. The physics-informed approach maintains high performance while achieving computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed machine learning (grey-box models) represent a promising approach for structural health monitoring that balances performance with reduced environmental impact. By leveraging physical insights alongside data, these models offer better extrapolation with lower computational costs, making them more sustainable alternatives to purely data-driven approaches.

title: Auditing Frontier Vision-Language Models for Trustworthy Medical VQA: Grounding Failures, Format Collapse, and Domain Adaptation
summary: Deploying vision-language models (VLMs) in clinical settings demands auditable behavior under realistic failure conditions, yet the failure landscape of frontier VLMs on specialized medical inputs is poorly characterized. We audit five recent frontier and grounding-aware VLMs (Gemini~2.5~Pro, GPT-5, o3, GLM-4.5V, Qwen~2.5~VL) on Medical VQA along two trust-relevant axes. Perception: all models localize anatomical and pathological targets poorly -- the best model reaches only 0.23 mean IoU and 19.1% Acc@0.5 -- and exhibit clinically dangerous laterality confusion. Pipeline integration: a self-grounding pipeline, where the same model localizes then answers, degrades VQA accuracy for every model -- driven by both inaccurate localization and format-compliance failures under the two-step prompt (parse failure rises to 70%--99% for Gemini and GPT-5 on VQA-RAD). Replacing predicted boxes with ground-truth annotations recovers and improves VQA accuracy, consistent with the failure residing in the perception module rather than in the decomposition itself. These observational findings identify grounding quality as a primary trustworthiness bottleneck in our SLAKE bounding-box setting. As a complementary fine-tuning follow-up, supervised fine-tuning of Qwen~2.5~VL on combined Med-VQA training data attains the highest reported SLAKE open-ended recall (85.5%) among comparable methods, suggesting that the VQA-level gap is tractable with domain adaptation; whether this also closes the perception/trustworthiness bottleneck is left to future work.

AI.tldr: Frontier VLMs fail at medical image localization and self-grounding, showing poor anatomical perception and pipeline integration issues, though VQA performance can be improved with domain-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Clinical deployment of vision-language models requires auditable behavior under failure conditions, but the failure landscape of frontier VLMs on medical inputs is poorly characterized.
AI.method: Audited five frontier VLMs (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-5, o3, GLM-4.5V, Qwen 2.5 VL) on Medical VQA along two axes: perception (localization quality) and pipeline integration (self-grounding performance). Also conducted fine-tuning experiments with Qwen 2.5 VL on medical VQA data.
AI.result: Models localize poorly (best: 0.23 mean IoU, 19.1% Acc@0.5) with dangerous laterality confusion. Self-grounding pipelines degrade VQA accuracy due to inaccurate localization and format-compliance failures. Replacing predicted boxes with ground truth recovers accuracy, confirming perception module failure. Fine-tuned Qwen 2.5 VL achieved highest SLAKE open-ended recall (85.5%).
AI.conclusion: Grounding quality is a primary trustworthiness bottleneck in medical VLM deployment. While VQA performance can be improved with domain adaptation, perception/trustworthiness issues remain unsolved, highlighting the need for specialized clinical evaluation benchmarks.

title: ANCORA: Learning to Question via Manifold-Anchored Self-Play for Verifiable Reasoning
summary: We propose a paradigm shift from learning to answer to learning to question: can a language model generate verifiable problems, solve them, and turn the resulting feedback into self-improvement without human supervision? We introduce ANCORA, an anchored-curriculum framework in which a unified policy alternates between a Proposer that synthesizes novel specifications and a Solver that produces verified solutions. ANCORA rests on three load-bearing mechanisms: a two-level group-relative update that couples Proposer advantages across specifications with Solver advantages across solution attempts; iterative self-distilled SFT that projects the base model onto its valid-output manifold before RL; and a UCB-guided Curriculum DAG that grows only through strictly filtered, novel, Solver-verified specifications. These stabilizers are necessary because sparse verifier feedback otherwise drives Proposer collapse even under MLRL-aligned rewards.
