Instantiated in Verus, ANCORA lifts Dafny2Verus pass@1 from a 26.6% SFT baseline to 81.5% in the test-time-training setting under 0-shot evaluation, outperforming the PSV self-play baseline by 15.8 points despite PSV using 1-shot inference; in a separate transfer setting, training from Dafny2Verus seeds yields 36.2% and 17.2% pass@1 on held-out MBPP and HumanEval.

AI.tldr: ANCORA is a framework that enables language models to self-improve by learning to generate verifiable problems, solve them, and use the feedback, outperforming baselines in program verification tasks without human supervision.
AI.motivation: The paper proposes a paradigm shift from teaching models to answer questions to teaching them to generate verifiable problems. The motivation is to enable language models to self-improve autonomously without human supervision by creating their own curriculum through problem generation and solution verification.
AI.method: ANCORA uses an anchored-curriculum framework with a unified policy alternating between: (1) Proposer that synthesizes novel specifications, (2) Solver that produces verified solutions. Key mechanisms include: two-level group-relative update coupling Proposer/Solver advantages, iterative self-distilled SFT projecting the base model onto valid-output manifold, and UCB-guided Curriculum DAG growing through filtered novel specifications.
AI.result: On Dafny2Verus, ANCORA lifts pass@1 from 26.6% SFT baseline to 81.5% in test-time training (0-shot), outperforming PSV self-play baseline by 15.8 points despite PSV using 1-shot inference. In transfer setting, training from Dafny2Verus seeds yields 36.2% pass@1 on held-out MBPP and 17.2% on HumanEval.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully enables language model self-improvement through autonomous problem generation and verification, demonstrating significant performance gains in program verification tasks and showing transferability to other benchmarks without human supervision.

title: Iterative Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Medical Question Answering
summary: Medical retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems typically operate on text chunks extracted from biomedical literature, discarding the rich visual content (tables, figures, structured layouts) of original document pages. We propose MED-VRAG, an iterative multimodal RAG framework that retrieves and reasons over PMC document page images instead of OCR'd text. The system pairs ColQwen2.5 patch-level page embeddings with a sharded MapReduce LLM filter, scaling to ~350K pages while keeping Stage-1 retrieval under 30 ms via an offline coarse-to-fine index (C=8 centroids per page, ANN over centroids, exact two-way scoring on the top-R shortlist). A vision-language model (VLM) then iteratively refines its query and accumulates evidence in a memory bank across up to 3 reasoning rounds, with a single iteration costing ~15.9 s and the full three-round pipeline ~47.8 s on 4xA100. Across four medical QA benchmarks (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU-Med), MEDVRAG reaches 78.6% average accuracy. Under controlled comparison with the same Qwen2.5-VL-32B backbone, retrieval contributes a +5.8 point gain over the no-retrieval baseline; we also note a +1.8 point edge over MedRAG + GPT-4 (76.8%), with the caveat that this is a cross-paper rather than head-to-head comparison. Ablations isolate +1.0 from page-image vs text-chunk retrieval, +1.5 from iteration, and +1.0 from the memory bank.

AI.tldr: MED-VRAG is a multimodal medical RAG system that retrieves and reasons over document page images rather than text chunks, achieving 78.6% average accuracy on medical QA benchmarks through iterative vision-language refinement.
AI.motivation: Medical RAG systems typically operate on text chunks extracted from biomedical literature, discarding rich visual content (tables, figures, structured layouts) of original document pages. This visual information is important for medical understanding but lost in text-only approaches.
AI.method: An iterative multimodal RAG framework that retrieves PMC document page images using ColQwen2.5 patch-level page embeddings with a sharded MapReduce LLM filter. Uses offline coarse-to-fine indexing (C=8 centroids per page, ANN over centroids, exact two-way scoring) for fast retrieval (under 30ms). Then a vision-language model iteratively refines queries and accumulates evidence in a memory bank across up to 3 reasoning rounds.
AI.result: Achieves 78.6% average accuracy across four medical QA benchmarks (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU-Med). Retrieval contributes +5.8 point gain over no-retrieval baseline, and outperforms MedRAG+GPT-4 by +1.8 points. Ablations show: +1.0 from page-image vs text-chunk retrieval, +1.5 from iteration, and +1.0 from memory bank.
AI.conclusion: MED-VRAG demonstrates the importance of preserving and utilizing visual content in medical document retrieval, with significant performance gains over text-only approaches through multimodal iterative reasoning.

title: When Does Structure Matter in Continual Learning? Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Shapes Representational Geometry
summary: To preserve previously learned representations, continual learning systems must strike a balance between plasticity, the ability to acquire new knowledge, and stability. This stability-plasticity dilemma affects how representations can be reused across tasks: shared structure enables transfer when tasks are similar but may also induce interference when new learning disrupts existing representations. However, it remains unclear when and why structural separation influences this trade-off. In this study, we examine how network architecture, task similarity, and representational dimensionality jointly shape learning in a sequential task paradigm inspired by transfer-interference studies. We compare a task-partitioned modular recurrent network with a single-module baseline by systematically varying task similarity (low, medium, high) and the scale of weight initialization, which induces different learning regimes that we empirically characterize through the effective dimensionality of the learned representations. We find that architecture has minimal impact in high-dimensional regimes where representations are sufficiently unconstrained to accommodate multiple tasks without strong interference. In contrast, in lower-dimensional (rich) regimes, architectural separation is decisive: modular networks exhibit graded alignment of task-specific subspaces with overlap for similar tasks, partial orthogonalization for moderately dissimilar tasks, and stronger separation for dissimilar tasks. This graded geometry is absent in the single network baseline. Our findings suggest that representational dimensionality acts as a key organizing variable governing when structural separation becomes functionally relevant, and highlight adaptive geometry as a central principle for designing continual learning systems.

AI.tldr: Representational dimensionality determines when architectural separation matters in continual learning: in high-dimensional regimes, modular vs single networks perform similarly, while in low-dimensional regimes, modular networks show graded task subspace alignment based on task similarity.
AI.motivation: To understand when and why structural separation (modular vs integrated architectures) influences the stability-plasticity trade-off in continual learning systems, particularly how this interacts with task similarity and representational constraints.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of task-partitioned modular recurrent networks vs single-module baselines, varying task similarity (low, medium, high) and weight initialization scale (inducing different effective dimensionality regimes), using a sequential task paradigm inspired by transfer-interference studies.
AI.result: Architecture has minimal impact in high-dimensional regimes where representations are flexible enough to accommodate multiple tasks without interference. In lower-dimensional regimes, modular networks show graded subspace geometry: aligned for similar tasks, partially orthogonalized for moderately dissimilar tasks, and strongly separated for dissimilar tasks - patterns absent in single networks.
AI.conclusion: Representational dimensionality is a key organizing variable that determines when structural separation becomes functionally relevant in continual learning, with adaptive subspace geometry emerging as a central principle for designing effective continual learning systems.

title: Consumer Attitudes Towards AI in Digital Health: A Mixed-Methods Survey in Australia
summary: AI applications are increasingly being introduced into digital health. While technical performance has advanced rapidly, successful deployment mainly depends on consumer attitudes, especially to patient-facing applications. However, most existing research examines consumer attitudes towards healthcare AI at an abstract level rather than in response to concrete artefacts. We report a mixed-methods survey study in Australia (N=275) examining consumer readiness, acceptance, trust, and risk perceptions of healthcare AI, combined with a scenario-based evaluation of an AI-generated versus clinician-written consultation summary. Participants expressed moderate optimism and strong perceived usefulness and ease of use, but also substantial concerns about accuracy, safety, and data use. In the scenario task, the AI-generated summary was strongly preferred for quality, empathy, and overall usefulness, yet identification of the AI summary was near chance. Findings show that consumers judge AI through concrete communication quality and visible human governance, underscoring the need for clinically supervised deployment frameworks beyond technical performance alone.

AI.tldr: Consumers strongly prefer AI-generated medical consultation summaries over clinician-written ones for quality and empathy, but can't reliably identify AI content, highlighting the importance of communication quality and human oversight in healthcare AI deployment.
AI.motivation: While AI technical performance has advanced rapidly in digital health, successful deployment depends on consumer attitudes, especially for patient-facing applications. Most existing research examines attitudes at an abstract level rather than in response to concrete AI artifacts.
AI.method: Mixed-methods survey study in Australia (N=275) examining consumer readiness, acceptance, trust, and risk perceptions of healthcare AI, combined with a scenario-based evaluation comparing AI-generated versus clinician-written consultation summaries.
AI.result: Participants expressed moderate optimism and strong perceived usefulness/ease of use, but had substantial concerns about accuracy, safety, and data use. In the scenario task, the AI-generated summary was strongly preferred for quality, empathy, and overall usefulness, yet identification of the AI summary was near chance level.
AI.conclusion: Consumers judge AI through concrete communication quality and visible human governance, underscoring the need for clinically supervised deployment frameworks beyond technical performance alone.

title: Optimized Deferral for Imbalanced Settings
summary: Learning algorithms can be significantly improved by routing complex or uncertain inputs to specialized experts, balancing accuracy with computational cost. This approach, known as learning to defer, is essential in domains like natural language generation, medical diagnosis, and computer vision, where an effective deferral can reduce errors at low extra resource consumption. However, the two-stage learning to defer setting, which leverages existing predictors such as a collection of LLMs or other classifiers, often faces challenges due to an expert imbalance problem. This imbalance can lead to suboptimal performance, with deferral algorithms favoring the majority expert. We present a comprehensive study of two-stage learning to defer in expert imbalance settings. We cast the deferral loss optimization as a novel cost-sensitive learning problem over the input-expert domain. We derive new margin-based loss functions and guarantees tailored to this setting, and develop novel algorithms for cost-sensitive learning. Leveraging these results, we design principled deferral algorithms, MILD (Margin-based Imbalanced Learning to Defer), specifically suited for expert imbalance settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, showing clear improvements over existing baselines on both image classification and real-world Large Language Model (LLM) routing tasks.

AI.tldr: Novel margin-based deferral algorithm MILD addresses expert imbalance in two-stage learning to defer systems, outperforming baselines on image classification and LLM routing tasks.
AI.motivation: Learning to defer can improve algorithms by routing complex inputs to specialized experts, but two-stage approaches suffer from expert imbalance problems where deferral algorithms favor majority experts, leading to suboptimal performance.
AI.method: Cast deferral loss optimization as cost-sensitive learning over input-expert domain, derive new margin-based loss functions with theoretical guarantees, develop cost-sensitive learning algorithms, and design principled MILD (Margin-based Imbalanced Learning to Defer) algorithm specifically for expert imbalance settings.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show clear improvements over existing baselines on both image classification and real-world Large Language Model (LLM) routing tasks, demonstrating effectiveness of the proposed approach.
AI.conclusion: The novel MILD algorithm effectively addresses expert imbalance in learning to defer systems, providing a principled solution with theoretical foundations and practical performance improvements across diverse application domains.

title: Mind the Gap: Structure-Aware Consistency in Preference Learning
summary: Preference learning has become the foundation of aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human intent. Popular methods, such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), minimize surrogate losses as proxies for the intractable pairwise ranking loss. However, we demonstrate that for the equicontinuous hypothesis sets typical of neural networks, these standard surrogates are theoretically inconsistent, yielding vacuous generalization guarantees. To resolve this, we formulate LLM alignment within a margin-shifted ranking framework. We derive rigorous $H$-consistency bounds that depend on enforcing a separation margin $γ$. Crucially, we extend this to Structure-Aware $H$-consistency, introducing a novel objective (SA-DPO) that adapts the margin based on the semantic distance between responses to handle synonyms and hard pairs. Finally, we analyze the trade-off between consistency and model limitations via the Margin-Capacity Profile, proving that heavy-tailed surrogates (such as the Polynomial Hinge family) offer superior consistency guarantees for capacity-bounded models compared to the standard logistic loss used in DPO.

AI.tldr: SA-DPO improves LLM alignment by introducing structure-aware margin scaling and heavy-tailed surrogates for better consistency and handling of semantic distance between responses.
AI.motivation: Current preference learning methods like DPO use surrogate losses that are theoretically inconsistent for neural network hypothesis sets, yielding vacuous generalization guarantees. There's a need to address the inconsistency and improve handling of semantic relationships between responses.
AI.method: Formulates LLM alignment within a margin-shifted ranking framework, derives rigorous H-consistency bounds with separation margin γ, introduces Structure-Aware H-consistency with SA-DPO objective that adapts margin based on semantic distance between responses, and analyzes trade-offs via Margin-Capacity Profile using heavy-tailed surrogates like Polynomial Hinge family.
AI.result: Heavy-tailed surrogates offer superior consistency guarantees for capacity-bounded models compared to standard logistic loss used in DPO. SA-DPO adapts margin scaling to handle synonyms and hard pairs based on semantic distance between responses.
AI.conclusion: The margin-shifted ranking framework with structure-aware margin adaptation and heavy-tailed surrogates provides theoretically consistent preference learning with better generalization guarantees for LLM alignment compared to existing methods like DPO.

title: Intent2Tx: Benchmarking LLMs for Translating Natural Language Intents into Ethereum Transactions
summary: The emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs) offers a transformative interface for Web3, yet existing benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of translating high-level user intents into functionally correct, state-dependent on-chain transactions. We present \textsc{Intent2Tx}, a high-fidelity benchmark featuring 29,921 single-step and 1,575 multi-step instances meticulously derived from 300 days of real-world Ethereum mainnet traces. Unlike prior works that rely on synthetic instructions, \textsc{Intent2Tx} grounds natural language intents in real-world protocol interactions across 11 categories, including diverse long-tail Decentralized Finance (DeFi) primitives. To enable rigorous evaluation, we propose an execution-aware framework that transcends surface-level text matching by employing differential state analysis on forked mainnet environments. Our extensive evaluation of 16 state-of-the-art LLMs reveals that while scaling and retrieval-augmentation enhance logical consistency and parameter precision, current models struggle with out-of-distribution generalization and multi-step planning. Crucially, our execution-based analysis demonstrates that syntactically valid outputs often fail to achieve intended state transitions, highlighting a significant gap in current "reasoning-to-execution" capabilities. \textsc{Intent2Tx} serves as a critical foundation for developing autonomous, reliable agents in intent-centric Web3 ecosystems. Code and data: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Intent2Tx_Bench-97FF .

AI.tldr: Intent2Tx is a benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to translate natural language intents into correct Ethereum transactions, using real-world data and execution-based validation to reveal significant gaps in current models' reasoning-to-execution capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current LLM benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of translating high-level user intents into functionally correct, state-dependent on-chain transactions for Web3 applications. There's a need for more realistic evaluation of LLMs' ability to bridge the gap between natural language reasoning and actual blockchain execution.
AI.method: Created Intent2Tx benchmark with 29,921 single-step and 1,575 multi-step instances derived from 300 days of real Ethereum mainnet traces across 11 protocol categories. Proposed an execution-aware framework using differential state analysis on forked mainnet environments, moving beyond surface-level text matching to evaluate actual state transitions.
AI.result: Evaluation of 16 state-of-the-art LLMs shows that while scaling and retrieval-augmentation improve logical consistency and parameter precision, models struggle with out-of-distribution generalization and multi-step planning. Crucially, execution-based analysis reveals that syntactically valid outputs often fail to achieve intended state transitions, exposing a significant gap in "reasoning-to-execution" capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Intent2Tx provides a critical foundation for developing autonomous, reliable agents in intent-centric Web3 ecosystems by offering a high-fidelity benchmark that captures the real-world complexity of translating user intents into executable blockchain transactions.

title: Differential Subgroup Discovery: Characterizing Where Two Populations Differ, and Why
summary: We study the problem of understanding where two populations differ within a feature space, which we formalize in the concept of a differential subgroup: a subset of individuals from both populations who, despite sharing similar characteristics, exhibit exceptional differences in a target outcome. Differential subgroups reveal the regions of the feature space where population-level gaps are most pronounced and can help practitioners identify the covariate combinations that are structurally responsible for these differences, e.g.~in clinical analysis, model diagnostics, or treatment-effect studies. We introduce a general optimization objective for discovering differential subgroups and establish conditions under which the resulting subgroups admit a causal interpretation of population differences. We propose DiffSub, a gradient-based approach that discovers interpretable differential subgroups in tabular data. Across synthetic benchmarks, medical case studies, model-error analyses, and treatment-effect settings, DiffSub identifies informative subgroups that reveal where population differences arise and why.

AI.tldr: DiffSub is a gradient-based method for discovering differential subgroups - subsets of individuals from two populations with similar characteristics but exceptional differences in target outcomes, revealing where population gaps are most pronounced.
AI.motivation: Need to identify where two populations differ within a feature space, specifically finding subgroups that share similar characteristics but exhibit exceptional outcome differences. This helps practitioners identify covariate combinations structurally responsible for population-level gaps in various domains like clinical analysis, model diagnostics, or treatment-effect studies.
AI.method: Introduced a general optimization objective for discovering differential subgroups and established conditions for causal interpretation. Proposed DiffSub, a gradient-based approach that discovers interpretable differential subgroups in tabular data.
AI.result: Across synthetic benchmarks, medical case studies, model-error analyses, and treatment-effect settings, DiffSub identifies informative subgroups that reveal where population differences arise and why.
AI.conclusion: Differential subgroups formalize the concept of identifying where population differences are most pronounced, and DiffSub provides an effective method for discovering these interpretable subgroups to understand the structural reasons behind population-level gaps.

title: WindowsWorld: A Process-Centric Benchmark of Autonomous GUI Agents in Professional Cross-Application Environments
summary: While GUI agents have shown impressive capabilities in common computer-use tasks such as OSWorld, current benchmarks mainly focus on isolated and single-application tasks. This overlooks a critical real-world requirement of coordinating across multiple applications to accomplish complex profession-specific workflows. To bridge this gap, we present a computer-use benchmark in cross-application workflows, named WindowsWorld, designed to systematically assess GUI Agents on complex multi-step tasks that mirror real-world professional activities. Our methodology uses a multi-agent framework steered by 16 occupations to generate four difficulty-level tasks with intermediate inspection, which are then refined by human review and executed in a simulated environment. The resulting benchmark contains 181 tasks with an average of 5.0 sub-goals across 17 common desktop applications, of which 78% are inherently multi-application. Experimental results of leading large models and agents show that: 1) All computer-use agents perform poorly on multi-application tasks (< 21% success rate), far below the performance of simple single-app tasks; 2) They largely fail at tasks requiring conditional judgment and reasoning across $\geq$ 3 applications, stalling at early sub-goals; 3) Low execution efficiency, where tasks often fail despite far exceeding human step limits. Code, benchmark data, and evaluation resources are available at github.com/HITsz-TMG/WindowsWorld.

AI.tldr: WindowsWorld is a new benchmark for GUI agents that tests multi-application workflows across 17 desktop applications, revealing current agents perform poorly on complex cross-application professional tasks compared to simple single-app tasks.
AI.motivation: Current GUI agent benchmarks focus too much on isolated, single-application tasks, overlooking the real-world need to coordinate across multiple applications to accomplish complex professional workflows.
AI.method: Uses multi-agent framework guided by 16 occupations to generate four difficulty-level tasks with intermediate inspection, refined by human review and executed in a simulated environment. Created 181 tasks with average 5.0 sub-goals across 17 common desktop applications.
AI.result: 1) All computer-use agents perform poorly on multi-application tasks (<21% success rate); 2) They fail at tasks requiring conditional judgment across ≥3 applications; 3) Low execution efficiency with tasks failing despite exceeding human step limits.
AI.conclusion: Current GUI agents struggle significantly with cross-application coordination needed for professional workflows, highlighting the need for benchmarks like WindowsWorld that better reflect real-world computer use requirements.

title: Linear-Core Surrogates: Smooth Loss Functions with Linear Rates for Classification and Structured Prediction
summary: The choice of loss function in classification involves a fundamental trade-off: smooth losses (like Cross-Entropy) enable fast optimization rates but yield slow square-root consistency bounds, while piecewise-linear losses (like Hinge) offer fast linear consistency rates but suffer from non-differentiability. We propose Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates, a new family of convex loss functions that resolve this tension by stitching a linear core to a smooth tail. We prove that these surrogates are differentiable everywhere while retaining strict linear $H$-consistency bounds, effectively combining the optimization benefits of smoothness with the statistical efficiency of margin-based losses. In the structured prediction setting, we show that this smoothness unlocks a massive computational and energy advantage: it allows for an unbiased stochastic gradient estimator that bypasses the quadratic complexity $O(|\mathscr{Y}|^2)$ of exact inference (e.g., Viterbi). Empirically, our method achieves a 23$\times$ speedup over Structured SVMs on large-vocabulary sequence tagging tasks and demonstrates superior robustness to instance-dependent label noise, outperforming Cross-Entropy by 2.6% on corrupted CIFAR-10.

AI.tldr: Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates combine smooth optimization with margin-based statistical efficiency, enabling differentiable losses with fast linear consistency rates and computational advantages in structured prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing loss functions in classification face a fundamental trade-off: smooth losses like Cross-Entropy enable fast optimization but have slow square-root consistency bounds, while piecewise-linear losses like Hinge offer fast linear consistency but suffer from non-differentiability and poor optimization properties.
AI.method: Proposes Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates, a new family of convex loss functions that stitch a linear core to a smooth tail, making them differentiable everywhere while retaining strict linear H-consistency bounds. This combines optimization benefits of smoothness with statistical efficiency of margin-based losses.
AI.result: 1) LC Surrogates achieve 23× speedup over Structured SVMs on large-vocabulary sequence tagging. 2) Superior robustness to instance-dependent label noise, outperforming Cross-Entropy by 2.6% on corrupted CIFAR-10. 3) Unbiased stochastic gradient estimator bypasses O(|Y|²) complexity of exact inference in structured prediction.
AI.conclusion: LC Surrogates resolve the fundamental trade-off between optimization efficiency and statistical consistency in classification losses, offering both smooth optimization and fast linear consistency with significant computational advantages in structured prediction tasks.

title: Post-Optimization Adaptive Rank Allocation for LoRA
summary: Exponential growth in the scale of modern foundation models has led to the widespread adoption of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique. However, standard LoRA implementations disregard the varying intrinsic dimensionality of model layers and enforce a uniform rank, leading to parameter redundancy. We propose Post-Optimization Adaptive Rank Allocation (PARA), a data-free compression method for LoRA that integrates seamlessly into existing fine-tuning pipelines. PARA leverages Singular Value Decomposition to prune LoRA ranks using a global threshold over singular values across all layers. This results in non-uniform rank allocation based on layer-wise spectral importance. As a post-hoc method, PARA circumvents the training modifications and resulting instabilities that dynamic architectures typically incur. We empirically demonstrate that PARA reduces parameter count by 75-90\% while preserving the predictive performance of the original, uncompressed LoRA across multiple vision and language benchmarks. Code will be published upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: PARA is a post-optimization, data-free compression method that uses global singular value thresholding to prune LoRA ranks non-uniformly, reducing parameters by 75-90% while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA implementations use uniform rank allocation across all model layers, ignoring that different layers have varying intrinsic dimensionality, which leads to parameter redundancy and inefficient use of compute resources in large foundation models.
AI.method: PARA uses Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to analyze LoRA adapters after training, then prunes ranks using a global threshold applied to singular values across all layers. This enables non-uniform rank allocation based on layer-wise spectral importance without requiring data or modifying the training process.
AI.result: PARA achieves 75-90% reduction in parameter count while preserving the predictive performance of original uncompressed LoRA across multiple vision and language benchmarks, outperforming uniform rank allocation approaches.
AI.conclusion: PARA provides an effective, practical compression solution for LoRA that integrates seamlessly into existing pipelines, eliminating parameter redundancy through adaptive rank allocation based on spectral analysis, without the instability issues of dynamic architectures during training.

title: On the Expressive Power of GNNs to Solve Linear SDPs
summary: Semidefinite programs (SDPs) are a powerful framework for convex optimization and for constructing strong relaxations of hard combinatorial problems. However, solving large SDPs can be computationally expensive, motivating the use of machine learning models as fast computational surrogates. Graph neural networks (GNNs) are a natural candidate in this setting due to their sparsity-awareness and ability to model variable-constraint interactions. In this work, we study what expressive power is sufficient to recover optimal SDP solutions. We first prove negative results showing that standard GNN architectures fail on recovering linear SDP solutions. We then identify a more expressive architecture that captures the key structure of SDPs and can, in particular, emulate the updates of a standard first-order solver. Empirically, on both synthetic and \textsc{SdpLib} benchmarks of various classes of SDPs, this more expressive architecture achieves consistently lower prediction error and objective gap than theoretically weaker baselines. Finally, using the learned high-quality predictions to warm-start the first-order solver yields practical speedups of up to 80%.

AI.tldr: GNNs as fast surrogates for SDPs: Standard GNNs fail to recover SDP solutions, but a more expressive architecture mimicking first-order solvers achieves lower prediction error and enables 80% solver speedups through warm-starting.
AI.motivation: Solving large semidefinite programs (SDPs) is computationally expensive, motivating the need for fast computational surrogates. Graph neural networks (GNNs) are natural candidates due to their sparsity-awareness and ability to model variable-constraint interactions.
AI.method: The paper first proves negative results showing standard GNN architectures fail at recovering linear SDP solutions. Then identifies a more expressive architecture that captures key SDP structure and can emulate updates of standard first-order solvers. Empirical evaluation is conducted on synthetic and SdpLib benchmarks across various SDP classes.
AI.result: The more expressive architecture achieves consistently lower prediction error and objective gap than theoretically weaker baselines. Using learned high-quality predictions to warm-start the first-order solver yields practical speedups of up to 80%.
AI.conclusion: A sufficiently expressive GNN architecture that captures SDP structure and mimics solver dynamics can serve as an effective surrogate for SDPs, enabling both accurate solution approximation and practical computational speedups through warm-starting.

title: Focus Session: Autonomous Systems Dependability in the era of AI: Design Challenges in Safety, Security, Reliability and Certification
summary: The design of embedded safety-critical systems such as those used in next-generation automotive and autonomous platforms, is increasingly challenged by escalating system complexity, hardware-software heterogeneity, and the integration of intelligent, data-driven components. Ensuring dependability in such systems requires a holistic approach that spans multiple abstraction layers and encompasses both design- and run-time assurance. Traditional methods for reliability, safety, and security management often fall short in addressing the dynamic and uncertain behaviors introduced by Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) components, especially under stringent real-time, power, and safety constraints. While AI and ML offer powerful predictive, adaptive, and self-optimizing capabilities that can enhance system dependability, their inherent non-determinism, data-dependence, and lack of formal guarantees introduce new challenges for verification, validation, and certification. This paper explores emerging methodologies, architectures, and frameworks for designing dependable autonomous and embedded systems in the era of AI. It highlight advances in reliability modeling, secure system design, and certification approaches that account for imperfect, learning-enabled components, aiming to bridge the gap between AI innovation and certifiable system-level dependability.

AI.tldr: This paper explores methodologies and frameworks for designing dependable AI-enabled safety-critical systems, addressing challenges of integrating intelligent components while maintaining reliability, safety, and security certification.
AI.motivation: Traditional reliability, safety, and security methods fall short when addressing dynamic AI/ML components in safety-critical systems, especially under real-time, power, and safety constraints. There's a growing need for holistic approaches that can handle AI's non-determinism and data-dependence while maintaining certifiable dependability.
AI.method: The paper explores emerging methodologies, architectures, and frameworks that span multiple abstraction layers, encompassing both design- and run-time assurance approaches. It examines advances in reliability modeling, secure system design, and certification approaches that account for imperfect, learning-enabled components.
AI.result: The research highlights advances that bridge the gap between AI innovation and certifiable system-level dependability, providing approaches to manage the challenges introduced by AI/ML components in safety-critical systems.
AI.conclusion: A holistic, multi-layer approach is essential for designing dependable autonomous and embedded systems with AI components, requiring new methodologies that can handle AI's uncertainties while meeting stringent safety and certification requirements.

title: Hyper-Dimensional Fingerprints as Molecular Representations
summary: Computational molecular representations underpin virtual screening, property prediction, and materials discovery. Conventional fingerprints are efficient and deterministic but lose structural information through hash-based compression, particularly at low dimensionalities. Learned representations from graph neural networks recover this expressiveness but require task-specific training and substantial computational resources. Here we introduce hyperdimensional fingerprints (HDF), which replace the learned transformations of message-passing neural networks with algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors, producing deterministic molecular representations without any training. Across diverse property prediction benchmarks, HDF outperforms conventional fingerprints in the majority of tasks while exhibiting greater consistency across datasets and models. Crucially, HDF embeddings preserve molecular similarity faithfully: at 32 dimensions, distances in HDF space achieve a 0.9 Pearson correlation with graph edit distance, compared to 0.55 for Morgan fingerprints at equivalent size. This structural fidelity persists at low dimensions where hash-based methods degrade, allowing simple nearest-neighbor regression to remain predictive with as few as 64 components. We further demonstrate the practical impact in Bayesian molecular optimization, where HDF-based surrogate models achieve substantially improved sample efficiency in regimes where Morgan fingerprints perform comparably to random search. HDF thus provides a general-purpose, training-free alternative to conventional molecular fingerprints, suggesting that the information loss long accepted as inherent to fixed-length fingerprints is a limitation of the hash-based encoding scheme rather than the fingerprint paradigm itself.

AI.tldr: HDF is a training-free molecular fingerprint using algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors instead of hash-based compression, preserving structural information better than conventional fingerprints.
AI.motivation: Conventional molecular fingerprints lose structural information through hash-based compression, especially at low dimensions, while learned representations from graph neural networks require task-specific training and significant computational resources.
AI.method: Replace learned transformations of message-passing neural networks with algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors to create deterministic molecular representations without any training (hyperdimensional fingerprints - HDF).
AI.result: HDF outperforms conventional fingerprints in most property prediction tasks, preserves molecular similarity better (0.9 vs 0.55 Pearson correlation with graph edit distance at 32 dimensions), maintains predictive performance at low dimensions, and improves sample efficiency in Bayesian molecular optimization.
AI.conclusion: HDF provides a general-purpose, training-free alternative to conventional molecular fingerprints, showing that information loss in fixed-length fingerprints is due to hash-based encoding limitations rather than being inherent to the fingerprint paradigm itself.

title: MCPHunt: An Evaluation Framework for Cross-Boundary Data Propagation in Multi-Server MCP Agents
comment: 21 pages, 1 figure, 16 tables. Code: https://github.com/lihaonan0716/MCPHunt Data: https://huggingface.co/datasets/lihaonan0716/mcphunt-agent-traces
summary: Multi-server MCP agents create an information-flow control problem: faithful tool composition can turn individually benign read/write permissions into cross-boundary credential propagation -- a structural side effect of workflow topology, not necessarily malicious model behavior. We present MCPHunt, to our knowledge the first controlled benchmark that isolates non-adversarial, verbatim credential propagation across multi-server MCP trust boundaries, with three methodological contributions: (1) canary-based taint tracking that reduces propagation detection to objective string matching; (2) an environment-controlled coverage design with risky, benign, and hard-negative conditions that validates pipeline soundness and controls for credential-format confounds; (3) CRS stratification that disentangles task-mandated propagation (faithful execution of verbatim-transfer instructions) from policy-violating propagation (credentials included despite the option to redact). Across 3,615 main-benchmark traces from 5 models spanning 147 tasks and 9 mechanism families, policy-violating propagation rates reach 11.5--41.3% across all models. This propagation is pathway-specific (25x cross-mechanism range) and concentrated in browser-mediated data flows; hard-negative controls provide evidence that production-format credentials are not necessary -- prompt-directed cross-boundary data flow is sufficient. A prompt-mitigation study across 3 models reduces policy-violating propagation by up to 97% while preserving 80.5% utility, but effectiveness varies with instruction-following capability -- suggesting that prompt-level defenses alone may not suffice. Code, traces, and labeling pipeline are released under MIT and CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: MCPHunt benchmark reveals non-adversarial credential propagation vulnerabilities in multi-server MCP agents, with policy-violating propagation rates reaching 11.5-41.3% across tested models, concentrated in browser-mediated flows.
AI.motivation: Multi-server MCP agents create information-flow control problems where faithful tool composition can inadvertently enable cross-boundary credential propagation - a structural side effect of workflow topology rather than malicious model behavior.
AI.method: MCPHunt introduces a controlled benchmark with: (1) canary-based taint tracking reducing detection to objective string matching, (2) environment-controlled coverage design with risky/benign/hard-negative conditions, (3) CRS stratification disentangling task-mandated vs policy-violating propagation.
AI.result: Across 3,615 traces from 5 models spanning 147 tasks and 9 mechanism families, policy-violating propagation rates reached 11.5-41.3% across models, with 25x cross-mechanism range. Propagation is concentrated in browser-mediated flows, and prompt-directed cross-boundary data flow alone is sufficient.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-level mitigations can reduce policy-violating propagation by up to 97% while preserving 80.5% utility, but effectiveness varies with instruction-following capability, suggesting prompt-level defenses alone may not suffice for security.

title: Probabilistic Circuits for Irregular Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Joint probabilistic modeling is essential for forecasting irregular multivariate time series (IMTS) to accurately quantify uncertainty. Existing approaches often struggle to balance model expressivity with consistent marginalization, frequently leading to unreliable or contradictory forecasts. To address this, we propose CircuITS, a novel architecture for probabilistic IMTS forecasting based on probabilistic circuits. Our model is flexible in capturing intricate dependencies between time series channels while structurally guaranteeing valid joint distributions. Experiments on four real world datasets demonstrate that CircuITS achieves superior joint and marginal density estimation compared to state of the art baselines.

AI.tldr: CircuITS is a novel probabilistic circuit-based architecture for irregular multivariate time series forecasting that guarantees valid joint distributions while capturing complex dependencies.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for irregular multivariate time series (IMTS) forecasting struggle to balance model expressivity with consistent marginalization, leading to unreliable or contradictory uncertainty quantification in joint probabilistic modeling.
AI.method: The authors propose CircuITS, a novel architecture based on probabilistic circuits that provides structural guarantees for valid joint distributions while maintaining flexibility to capture intricate dependencies between time series channels.
AI.result: Experiments on four real-world datasets show that CircuITS achieves superior joint and marginal density estimation compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: CircuITS effectively addresses the trade-off between expressivity and consistent marginalization in IMTS forecasting, providing reliable probabilistic forecasts with valid joint distributions.

title: CastFlow: Learning Role-Specialized Agentic Workflows for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown great promise in time series forecasting. However, most existing LLM-based forecasting methods still follow a static generative paradigm that directly maps historical observations to future values in a single pass. Under this paradigm, forecasting is constrained by limited temporal pattern extraction, single-round acquisition of contextual features, one-shot forecast generation, and lack of support from ensemble forecasts. To address these limitations, in this work, we propose CastFlow, a dynamic agentic forecasting framework that enables multi-view temporal pattern extraction, multi-round contextual features acquisition, iterative forecast refinement, and forecasting with ensemble forecasts. First, CastFlow organizes the forecasting process into planning, action, forecasting, and reflection, establishing an agentic workflow. Second, this workflow is supported by a memory module that retrieves prior experience and a multi-view toolkit that constructs diagnostic evidence and provides a reliable ensemble forecast baseline. Third, CastFlow adopts a role-specialized design that combines general-purpose reasoning with specialized numerical forecasting. Under this design, a frozen LLM preserves general-purpose reasoning, while a fine-tuned domain-specific LLM performs evidence-guided numerical forecasting based on the ensemble forecast baseline, rather than from scratch. To optimize a fine-tuned domain-specific LLM, we further develop a two-stage workflow-oriented training that combines supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). To evaluate the effectiveness of CastFlow, we conduct extensive experiments on diverse datasets and show that it achieves superior overall results against strong baselines. We hope that this work can serve as a step toward more adaptive and accurate time series forecasting.

AI.tldr: CastFlow introduces an agentic framework for LLM-based time series forecasting with multi-view pattern extraction, iterative refinement, and ensemble support, achieving superior results.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based forecasting methods follow static generative paradigms with limitations: limited temporal pattern extraction, single-round contextual feature acquisition, one-shot forecast generation, and lack of ensemble forecast support.
AI.method: Proposes CastFlow with: 1) Agentic workflow organized into planning, action, forecasting, and reflection; 2) Memory module and multi-view toolkit for diagnostic evidence and ensemble baselines; 3) Role-specialized design combining frozen LLM (general reasoning) with fine-tuned domain-specific LLM (evidence-guided forecasting); 4) Two-stage workflow-oriented training combining SFT and RL with verifiable rewards.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse datasets show CastFlow achieves superior overall results against strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: CastFlow represents a step toward more adaptive and accurate time series forecasting by addressing limitations of static LLM-based forecasting methods through dynamic agentic frameworks.

title: A Grid-Aware Agent-Based Model for Analyzing Electric Vehicle Charging Systems
comment: This is the author's version of a paper submitted to SIMULTECH. 12 Pages, 1 Table, 10 Figures
summary: This paper presents a configurable, grid-aware Agent-Based Model (ABM) for the systematic analysis of electric vehicle (EV) charging systems under configurable infrastructure and operational conditions. The model integrates heterogeneous EV behavior, charging column constraints, and a shared Energy Sandbox that regulates aggregate power allocation, enabling the joint study of user-centric charging dynamics and facility-level power behavior. Implemented in Python using the SimPy discrete-event framework, the approach supports scalable, event-driven simulations across varying system sizes, charger compositions, and scheduling strategies. A representative workplace charging scenario is investigated to illustrate how infrastructure configuration and coordination mechanisms influence energy delivery performance, infrastructure utilization, and aggregate load characteristics. The results highlight the context-dependence of infrastructure suitability and demonstrate how charging strategies and charger types reshape both service-level outcomes and grid-facing behavior. The proposed ABM provides a flexible and extensible simulation environment for exploring technical, operational, and grid-aware aspects of EV charging ecosystems, and for serving as a methodological basis for subsequent studies on advanced coordination strategies beyond the specific scenario analyzed in this study.

AI.tldr: A configurable grid-aware ABM for analyzing EV charging systems that studies how infrastructure and operational conditions affect charging dynamics and grid behavior.
AI.motivation: To enable systematic analysis of EV charging systems under various infrastructure and operational conditions, allowing joint study of user-centric charging dynamics and facility-level power behavior for better understanding of EV charging ecosystems.
AI.method: Developed a configurable Agent-Based Model (ABM) implemented in Python using SimPy discrete-event framework that integrates heterogeneous EV behavior, charging column constraints, and a shared Energy Sandbox for power allocation.
AI.result: Demonstrated through workplace charging scenarios how infrastructure configuration and coordination mechanisms influence energy delivery performance, infrastructure utilization, and aggregate load characteristics, showing context-dependent infrastructure suitability.
AI.conclusion: The ABM provides a flexible simulation environment for exploring technical, operational, and grid-aware aspects of EV charging, serving as a methodological basis for future studies on advanced coordination strategies.

title: Physical Foundation Models: Fixed hardware implementations of large-scale neural networks
summary:
Foundation models are deep neural networks (such as GPT-5, Gemini~3, and Opus~4) trained on large datasets that can perform diverse downstream tasks -- text and code generation, question answering, summarization, image classification, and so on. The philosophy of foundation models is to put effort into a single, large (${\sim}10^{12}$-parameter) general-purpose model that can be adapted to many downstream tasks with no or minimal additional training. We argue that the rise of foundation models presents an opportunity for hardware engineers: in contrast to when different models were used for different tasks, it now makes sense to build special-purpose, fixed hardware implementations of neural networks, manufactured and released at the roughly 1-year cadence of major new foundation-model versions.
  Beyond conventional digital-electronic inference hardware with read-only weight memory, we advocate a more radical re-thinking: hardware in which the neural network is realized directly at the level of the physical design and operates via the hardware's natural physical dynamics -- \textit{Physical Foundation Models} (PFMs). PFMs could enable orders-of-magnitude advantages in energy efficiency, speed, and parameter density. For ${\sim}10^{12}$-parameter models, this would both reduce the high energy burden of AI in datacenters and enable AI in edge devices that today are power-constrained to far smaller models. PFMs could also enable inference hardware for models much larger than current ones: $10^{15}$- or even $10^{18}$-parameter PFMs seem plausible by some measures. We present back-of-the-envelope calculations illustrating PFM scaling using an optical example -- a 3D nanostructured glass medium -- and discuss prospects in nanoelectronics and other physical platforms. We conclude with the major research challenges that must be resolved for trillion-parameter PFMs and beyond to become reality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Physical Foundation Models (PFMs) - hardware implementations of neural networks using physical systems' natural dynamics instead of digital electronics, promising massive efficiency gains for trillion-parameter AI models.
AI.motivation: The rise of foundation models (large general-purpose AI models) presents an opportunity to build special-purpose hardware implementations. Current digital electronic inference hardware has limitations in energy efficiency, speed, and parameter density for massive (~10^12 parameter) models, creating high energy burdens in datacenters and limiting AI deployment in power-constrained edge devices.
AI.method: The authors advocate for hardware where neural networks are realized directly at the physical design level, operating via the hardware's natural physical dynamics rather than conventional digital electronics with read-only weight memory. They present back-of-the-envelope calculations illustrating PFM scaling using an optical example - a 3D nanostructured glass medium - and discuss prospects in nanoelectronics and other physical platforms.
AI.result: PFMs could enable orders-of-magnitude advantages in energy efficiency, speed, and parameter density compared to digital electronic implementations. For ~10^12-parameter models, this would reduce AI's energy burden in datacenters and enable AI in power-constrained edge devices. PFMs could also enable inference hardware for much larger models: 10^15- or even 10^18-parameter PFMs seem plausible by some measures.
AI.conclusion: Physical Foundation Models represent a radical re-thinking of AI hardware that could overcome current limitations of digital electronic implementations, enabling massive-scale AI models with unprecedented efficiency. The paper concludes by identifying major research challenges that must be resolved for trillion-parameter PFMs and beyond to become reality.

title: Rethinking Agentic Reinforcement Learning In Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has traditionally focused on training specialized agents to optimize predefined reward functions within narrowly defined environments. However, the advent of powerful Large Language Models (LLMs) and increasingly complex, open-ended tasks has catalyzed a paradigm shift towards agentic paradigms within RL. This emerging framework extends beyond traditional RL by emphasizing the development of autonomous agents capable of goal-setting, long-term planning, dynamic strategy adaptation, and interactive reasoning in uncertain, real-world environments. Unlike conventional approaches that rely heavily on static objectives and episodic interactions, LLM-based Agentic RL incorporates cognitive-like capabilities such as meta-reasoning, self-reflection, and multi-step decision-making directly into the learning loop. In this paper, we provide a deep insight for looking the conceptual foundations, methodological innovations, and effective designs underlying this trend. Furthermore, we identify critical challenges and outline promising future directions for building LLM-based Agentic RL.

AI.tldr: LLM-based Agentic RL represents a paradigm shift from traditional RL by incorporating cognitive-like capabilities like goal-setting, planning, and meta-reasoning into autonomous agents for complex, open-ended tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL is limited to specialized agents with predefined rewards in narrow environments. With the rise of powerful LLMs and complex real-world tasks, there's a need for more autonomous agents capable of handling uncertainty, long-term planning, and dynamic adaptation.
AI.method: The paper provides conceptual foundations and methodological innovations for LLM-based Agentic RL, which extends traditional RL by incorporating cognitive capabilities like meta-reasoning, self-reflection, and multi-step decision-making directly into the learning loop.
AI.result: The paper offers deep insights into the emerging framework of LLM-based Agentic RL, identifying effective designs that enable agents to handle goal-setting, long-term planning, dynamic strategy adaptation, and interactive reasoning in uncertain environments.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based Agentic RL represents a significant advancement over traditional RL, but critical challenges remain. The paper outlines promising future directions for building more capable autonomous agents that can operate effectively in complex, real-world scenarios.

title: Beyond the Baseband: Adaptive Multi-Band Encoding for Full-Spectrum Bioacoustics Classification
summary: Animals hear and vocalize across frequency ranges that differ substantially from humans, often extending into the ultrasonic domain. Yet most computational bioacoustics systems rely on audio models pre-trained at 16 kHz, restricting their usable bandwidth to the 0-8 kHz baseband and discarding higher-frequency information present in many bioacoustic recordings. We investigate a multi-band encoding framework that decomposes the full spectrum of animal calls into band features and fuses them into a unified representation. Similarity analyses on models show that certain encoders produce decorrelated band embeddings that improve class separation after fusion. Classification experiments on three bioacoustic datasets using eight pre-trained models and five fusion strategies show that fused representations consistently outperform the baseband and time-expansion baselines on two datasets, showing the potential of multi-band methods for full-spectrum encoding of animal calls.

AI.tldr: Multi-band encoding framework improves bioacoustic classification by processing full-spectrum animal calls beyond standard 0-8 kHz baseband, outperforming traditional baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard bioacoustics systems use audio models pre-trained at 16 kHz, restricting usable bandwidth to 0-8 kHz baseband and discarding higher-frequency ultrasonic information present in many animal vocalizations.
AI.method: Propose a multi-band encoding framework that decomposes full-spectrum animal calls into band features and fuses them into unified representations. Test eight pre-trained models and five fusion strategies on three bioacoustic datasets.
AI.result: Fused representations consistently outperform baseband and time-expansion baselines on two datasets. Certain encoders produce decorrelated band embeddings that improve class separation after fusion.
AI.conclusion: Multi-band methods show strong potential for full-spectrum encoding of animal calls, enabling better utilization of ultrasonic frequency information beyond traditional baseband approaches in computational bioacoustics.

title: KellyBench: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Sequential Decision Making
summary: Language models are saturating benchmarks for procedural tasks with narrow objectives. But they are increasingly being deployed in long-horizon, non-stationary environments with open-ended goals. In this paper we introduce KellyBench, an environment for evaluating sequential decision-making in sports betting markets. Agents are placed in a sequential simulation of the 2023-24 English Premier League season and tasked with maximising their long-term bankroll growth. They are given detailed historical data, including advanced statistics, lineups, and public odds. To succeed they must build machine learning models, identify edge in public markets, and adapt as the environment changes over time. We find that all frontier models evaluated lose money on average over the course of the season for five seeds. The best performing model achieves an average return of -8%, and many models experiencing ruin across seeds. To judge strategy sophistication, we use a human expert rubric to grade each model and find their approaches to be unsophisticated compared to human baselines; Claude Opus 4.6 achieves a rubric score of 26.5%, which means there is significant room for improvement. KellyBench is available as an open-access API endpoint at https://openreward.ai/GeneralReasoning/KellyBench.

AI.tldr: KellyBench is a sports betting environment for evaluating LLMs' sequential decision-making; frontier models lose money on average, showing they struggle with long-horizon financial decision-making.
AI.motivation: Current language models excel at narrow procedural tasks but perform poorly in long-horizon, non-stationary environments with open-ended goals like financial decision-making.
AI.method: Created KellyBench, a sequential simulation of the 2023-24 English Premier League season where agents must maximize bankroll growth using historical data, advanced statistics, lineups, and public odds.
AI.result: All frontier models lost money on average across five seeds; best model had -8% return, many experienced ruin. Human expert rubric showed models' approaches were unsophisticated (Claude Opus 4.6 scored 26.5%).
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are inadequate for complex sequential financial decision-making; KellyBench provides an open-access benchmark to drive improvement in long-horizon reasoning and adaptation.

title: Calibrating Attribution Proxies for Reward Allocation in Participatory Weather Sensing
summary: Large-scale IoT weather sensing networks require incentive mechanisms to sustain participation, yet determining how much value individual data contributions bring to the network remains an open problem. Existing approaches address data quality but not data valuation; in operational meteorology, adjoint-based methods derive value from the forecast model itself but require full data assimilation infrastructure. We propose to utilise differentiable AI weather models to fill this gap and characterise gradient-based attribution on gridded GFS analysis inputs as a candidate value signal, evaluating fidelity, calibration, cost, and gaming vulnerability across more than 400 configurations. Attribution captures near-optimal sensor placement utility with monotonically faithful payments, but can be inflated by adversarial inputs, with detection requiring external baseline data. These findings establish gradient attribution as a computationally validated signal for model-informed reward allocation in participatory weather sensing.

AI.tldr: Differentiable AI weather models are used to compute gradient-based attribution as a value signal for data contributions in IoT weather sensing networks, offering a computationally efficient alternative to traditional adjoint-based methods.
AI.motivation: Large-scale IoT weather sensing networks need incentive mechanisms but determining the value of individual data contributions is challenging. Existing approaches address data quality but not valuation, while operational meteorology's adjoint-based methods require complex data assimilation infrastructure.
AI.method: Proposes using differentiable AI weather models to compute gradient-based attribution on gridded GFS analysis inputs as a value signal. Evaluates fidelity, calibration, cost, and gaming vulnerability across more than 400 configurations.
AI.result: Attribution captures near-optimal sensor placement utility with monotonically faithful payments, but can be inflated by adversarial inputs requiring external baseline data for detection. Gradient attribution shows promise as a computationally validated signal.
AI.conclusion: Gradient attribution establishes itself as a computationally validated signal for model-informed reward allocation in participatory weather sensing networks, though adversarial vulnerability requires mitigation strategies.

title: Modeling Clinical Concern Trajectories in Language Model Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents deployed in clinical settings often exhibit abrupt, threshold-driven behavior, offering little visibility into accumulating risk prior to escalation. In real-world care, however, clinicians act on gradually rising concern rather than instantaneous triggers. We study whether explicit state dynamics can expose such pre-escalation signals without delegating clinical authority to the agent. We introduce a lightweight agent architecture in which a memoryless clinical risk encoder is integrated over time using first- and second-order dynamics to produce a continuous escalation pressure signal. Across synthetic ward scenarios, stateless agents exhibit sharp escalation cliffs, while second-order dynamics produce smooth, anticipatory concern trajectories despite similar escalation timing. These trajectories surface sustained unease prior to escalation, enabling human-in-the-loop monitoring and more informed intervention. Our results suggest that explicit state dynamics can make LLM agents more clinically legible by revealing how long concern has been rising, not just when thresholds are crossed.

AI.tldr: LLM agents in clinical settings show abrupt threshold-driven behavior, but clinicians work on gradual concern. The paper introduces a memoryless risk encoder with state dynamics to produce continuous escalation pressure signals that reveal pre-escalation concern trajectories.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents in clinical settings exhibit abrupt, threshold-driven behavior that lacks visibility into accumulating risk before escalation. This contrasts with real-world clinical practice where clinicians act on gradually rising concern, creating a need for more transparent and anticipatory agent behavior.
AI.method: Introduces a lightweight agent architecture with a memoryless clinical risk encoder integrated over time using first- and second-order dynamics to produce continuous escalation pressure signals. The approach enables human-in-the-loop monitoring by surfacing concern trajectories prior to escalation thresholds.
AI.result: In synthetic ward scenarios, stateless agents exhibit sharp escalation cliffs, while second-order dynamics produce smooth, anticipatory concern trajectories despite similar escalation timing. The state dynamics reveal sustained unease prior to escalation, enabling better monitoring and intervention.
AI.conclusion: Explicit state dynamics can make LLM agents more clinically legible by revealing how long concern has been rising, not just when thresholds are crossed, enabling more informed human oversight and intervention in clinical settings.

title: Differentiable latent structure discovery for interpretable forecasting in clinical time series
comment: This manuscript is under review at BioData Mining
summary: Background: Timely, uncertainty-aware forecasting from irregular electronic health records (EHR) can support critical-care decisions, yet most approaches either impute to a grid or sacrifice interpretability. We introduce StructGP, a continuous-time multi-task Gaussian process that couples process convolutions with differentiable structure learning to uncover a sparse, ordered directed acyclic graph (DAG) of inter-variable dependencies while preserving principled uncertainty. We further propose LP-StructGP, which augments StructGP with latent pathways-shared, temporally shifted trajectories inferred via subject-specific coupling filters and a softmax gating mechanism-to capture cross-patient progression patterns. Both models are trained under sparsity and acyclicity constraints (augmented Lagrangian, Adam) using scalable low-rank updates. Results: In simulations, the approach reliably recovers ground-truth graphs (Structural Hamming Distance approaching 0 as cohorts grow) and pathway assignments (high Adjusted Rand Index). On a MIMIC-IV septic shock cohort (n=1,008; norepinephrine, creatinine, mean arterial pressure), StructGP improves short-horizon (6 h) forecasting over independent-task baselines (average RMSE 0.68 [95%CI: 0.63--0.74] vs. 0.88 [0.83-0.94]) and, with 15 additional inputs, markedly outperforms unstructured kernels (0.63 [0.58-0.69] vs. 3.02 [2.85-3.18]) with superior calibration (coverage 0.96 vs. 0.84). On the PhysioNet Challenge (12k patients, 41 variables), StructGP attains competitive accuracy (MAE 3.72e-2) relative to a state-of-the-art graph neural model while maintaining calibrated uncertainty. Conclusion: These results show that structured process convolutions with latent pathways deliver interpretable, scalable, and well-calibrated forecasting for irregular clinical time series.

AI.tldr: StructGP/LP-StructGP: Continuous-time Gaussian process models for irregular EHR data that learn sparse DAG structures and latent progression pathways for interpretable, uncertainty-aware forecasting.
AI.motivation: Need for timely, uncertainty-aware forecasting from irregular EHR data without sacrificing interpretability. Most existing approaches either impute data to a regular grid or use black-box models that lack interpretability.
AI.method: Two models: StructGP uses process convolutions with differentiable structure learning to uncover sparse ordered DAGs of variable dependencies. LP-StructGP adds latent pathways - shared temporally shifted trajectories with subject-specific coupling filters and softmax gating. Both use sparsity/acyclicity constraints, augmented Lagrangian optimization with Adam, and scalable low-rank updates.
AI.result: Recovers ground-truth graphs in simulations (SHD→0 with cohort growth). On MIMIC-IV septic shock data: StructGP improves 6h forecasting over baselines (RMSE 0.68 vs 0.88) and outperforms unstructured kernels (0.63 vs 3.02). On PhysioNet Challenge: competitive MAE (3.72e-2) vs state-of-the-art graph neural models while maintaining calibrated uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Structured process convolutions with latent pathways provide interpretable, scalable, and well-calibrated forecasting for irregular clinical time series, balancing accuracy with uncertainty quantification.

title: Building Persona-Based Agents On Demand: Tailoring Multi-Agent Workflows to User Needs
summary: Recent advances in agentic AI are shifting automation from discrete tools to proactive multi-agent systems that coordinate multi-specialized capabilities behind unified interfaces. However, today's agent systems typically rely on hard-coded agent architectures with fixed roles, coordination patterns, and interaction flows that limit end-user personalization and make adaptation to individual needs and contexts difficult. Given this limitation, we argue that on-demand persona-based agent generation offers a promising path towards more efficient and contextually appropriate interaction within agentic workflows. By dynamically crafting agents and personas at run-time to match user characteristics, task demands, and workflow context, agentic platforms can move beyond one-size-fits-all configurations. We present a pipeline for on-demand persona generation in agentic platforms, detailing how real-time crafting of AI personas can be systematically integrated within agent systems, aiming to open new possibilities in agentic platform design paradigms.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a pipeline for on-demand persona generation in agentic AI platforms, enabling dynamic creation of specialized agents tailored to individual user needs and contexts rather than using fixed, one-size-fits-all architectures.
AI.motivation: Current agentic AI systems rely on hard-coded architectures with fixed roles, coordination patterns, and interaction flows, which limit end-user personalization and make adaptation to individual needs and contexts difficult.
AI.method: The authors present a pipeline for on-demand persona generation in agentic platforms, detailing how real-time crafting of AI personas can be systematically integrated within agent systems to dynamically craft agents and personas at run-time.
AI.result: The proposed approach enables matching agent characteristics to user characteristics, task demands, and workflow context, moving beyond one-size-fits-all configurations toward more efficient and contextually appropriate interactions.
AI.conclusion: On-demand persona-based agent generation offers a promising path for more personalized and adaptive agentic workflows, with potential to open new possibilities in agentic platform design paradigms.

title: ITS-Mina: A Harris Hawks Optimization-Based All-MLP Framework with Iterative Refinement and External Attention for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multivariate time series forecasting plays a pivotal role in numerous real-world applications, including financial analysis, energy management, and traffic planning. While Transformer-based architectures have gained popularity for this task, recent studies reveal that simpler MLP-based models can achieve competitive or superior performance with significantly reduced computational cost. In this paper, we propose ITS-Mina, a novel all-MLP framework for multivariate time series forecasting that integrates three key innovations: (1) an iterative refinement mechanism that progressively enhances temporal representations by repeatedly applying a shared-parameter residual mixer stack, effectively deepening the model's computational capacity without multiplying the number of distinct parameters; (2) an external attention module that replaces traditional self-attention with learnable memory units, capturing cross-sample global dependencies at linear computational complexity; and (3) a Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm for automatic dropout rate tuning, enabling adaptive regularization tailored to each dataset. Extensive experiments on six widely-used benchmark datasets demonstrate that ITS-Mina achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance compared to eleven baseline models across multiple forecasting horizons.

AI.tldr: ITS-Mina is an all-MLP framework for multivariate time series forecasting that achieves state-of-the-art performance with three key innovations: iterative refinement mechanism, external attention module, and Harris Hawks Optimization for dropout tuning.
AI.motivation: While Transformer-based models are popular for multivariate time series forecasting, simpler MLP-based models can achieve competitive or superior performance with significantly reduced computational cost. The paper aims to develop an efficient all-MLP framework that overcomes traditional limitations.
AI.method: ITS-Mina integrates three innovations: 1) iterative refinement mechanism using shared-parameter residual mixer stack to deepen computational capacity without parameter multiplication; 2) external attention module with learnable memory units replacing self-attention for linear complexity global dependency capture; 3) Harris Hawks Optimization algorithm for automatic dropout rate tuning for adaptive regularization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets show ITS-Mina achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance compared to eleven baseline models across multiple forecasting horizons.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ITS-Mina framework demonstrates that efficient all-MLP architectures can achieve superior forecasting performance with reduced computational complexity, offering a promising alternative to Transformer-based models for multivariate time series forecasting tasks.

title: In-Context Prompting Obsoletes Agent Orchestration for Procedural Tasks
summary: Agent orchestration frameworks -- LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, and others -- place an external orchestrator above the LLM, tracking state and injecting routing instructions at every turn. We present a controlled comparison showing that for procedural tasks, this architecture is dominated by a simpler alternative: putting the entire procedure in the system prompt and letting the model self-orchestrate. Across three domains -- travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom technical support (14 nodes), and insurance claims processing (55 nodes) -- we evaluate 200 conversations per condition using LLM-as-judge scoring on five quality criteria. The in-context approach scores 4.53--5.00 on a 5-point scale while a LangGraph orchestrator using the same model scores 4.17--4.84. The orchestrated system fails on 24% of travel, 9% of Zoom, and 17% of insurance conversations, compared to 11.5%, 0.5%, and 5% for the in-context baseline. While external orchestration may have been necessary for earlier models, advances in frontier model capabilities have made it unnecessary for multi-turn conversations following a defined procedure.

AI.tldr: Putting entire procedures in system prompts (in-context self-orchestration) outperforms external orchestration frameworks (like LangGraph) for procedural tasks with modern LLMs.
AI.motivation: Agent orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, etc.) use external orchestrators to manage state and routing for LLMs, but there's a question of whether this complex architecture is actually necessary or optimal compared to simpler approaches.
AI.method: Conducted a controlled comparison across three domains (travel booking with 14 nodes, Zoom technical support with 14 nodes, and insurance claims processing with 55 nodes). Evaluated 200 conversations per condition using LLM-as-judge scoring on five quality criteria. Compared in-context approach (entire procedure in system prompt) vs. LangGraph orchestrator using the same model.
AI.result: In-context approach scored 4.53-5.00 on 5-point scale vs. 4.17-4.84 for LangGraph. Failure rates: 24% travel, 9% Zoom, 17% insurance for orchestrated vs. 11.5%, 0.5%, 5% for in-context baseline. Simpler approach consistently outperformed complex orchestration.
AI.conclusion: While external orchestration may have been necessary for earlier LLMs, advances in frontier model capabilities have made it unnecessary for multi-turn conversations following defined procedures. Simpler in-context self-orchestration is more effective.

title: Dynamic Scaled Gradient Descent for Stable Fine-Tuning for Classifications
summary: Fine-tuning pretrained models has become a standard approach to adapting pretrained knowledge to improve the accuracy on new sparse, imbalance datasets. However, issues arise when optimization falls into a collapsed state, where the model gets stuck, leading to degraded performance and unstable training. One possible reason for this is the cancellation of gradients across training examples. To address this problem, we propose a novel algorithm, dynamic scaled gradient descent (\mName), that directly modifies the gradients returned by training examples, specifically, scaling down the gradients of correctly classified examples using a dynamic scaler. This strategy offers both theoretical and empirical advantages in improving training stability. Experiments on a variety of benchmark datasets, spanning multiple tasks and large pretrained models, demonstrate that our method consistently reduces performance variance and surpasses the accuracy of existing approaches.

AI.tldr: Proposes dynamic scaled gradient descent (DSGD) to prevent optimization collapse during fine-tuning by scaling down gradients of correctly classified examples, improving stability and accuracy across multiple tasks.
